For your Taxpayer Identification Number
For Entity Customers:
The country in which your entity was set up
Whether your entity is a Financial Institution
How your entity generates its income
The Country(s) of Citizenship and Country(s) of Foreign Tax Residence for any controlling individuals linked to your entity
What information is forwarded on to the New Zealand IRD?
Name
Address
Date of birth
Taxpayer Identification Number
Account numbers
Account balances
Interest payments
We will only forward information onto the IRD if it is established that our customer is a US person, a US entity, a certain type of New Zealand entity controlled by US persons, or if a customer does not respond in full to our request for information in relation to FATCA.
What happens if you don’t respond to a financial institution’s FATCA request?
If we do not receive a response from you, we are required to report your non-response, your identity and reportable balance to the New Zealand IRD, who will in turn pass this information to the IRS.
If you have questions about FATCA you can also give our Customer Verification Services team a call on
0800 111 373
(or +64 9 337 4564 if calling from overseas) or via email on CustomerVerificationServices@asb.co.nz.
If you receive a FATCA request from us then please respond.
If you are unsure about your US tax obligations we recommend you visit the
Inland Revenue‘s website
or seek professional advice from an independent tax advisor.

question: Can ASB move money from one of my accounts to another without my authorisation?
answer:
Yes, we can use money in your accounts to repay any money you owe us. For example, if you're overdue on payments to your loans, credit cards or overdrafts.
In this case, we may use money from any account in your name - including joint accounts - to reduce or repay what you owe.
To do this ASB may:
Transfer money from one account to another;
Treat any number of accounts as one;
Use money in one currency to buy money in another currency; and
Break term deposits
If this happens we’ll let you know, but it’s possible you’ll get no prior notice.

question: Can ASB decline to offer products or services to me?
answer:
Yes. Some of ASB's products, accounts and services have specific eligibility requirements. As some people do not meet these requirements, we can't offer the services and products to everyone.
If ASB does decline a service or product, we’re not obliged to explain why a customer does not qualify.

question: Can ASB close my account?
answer:
Yes, ASB may close an account, or end any service by writing to you and giving you 14 days' notice.
We may close an account, or all accounts, immediately if:
You are, or ASB reasonably considers you may be using or obtaining, or allowing someone else to use or obtain, money or a service illegally, acting fraudulently or unlawfully, or behaving improperly (for example, in a threatening or abusive manner);
ASB reasonably considers that by continuing the agreement ASB may breach a court order or other legal requirement; or
you have broken the terms and conditions in any other way.

question: Can ASB apply a hold to my joint account if the other party to the account instructs them?
answer:
Yes. If there is a dispute between joint account holders one, or all, may notify ASB. Once we've been notified, we may take steps to prevent any account holder from using the account until the dispute has ended.
We may also suspend the operation of your account to protect any third parties who have a reasonable claim of interest in the account.

question: How do I cancel my unwanted credit card?
answer:
Cancelling your credit card is a little different from closing your credit card account entirely. For example, you may wish to cancel your card, but leave your credit card account open in order to make regular repayments on the outstanding balance.
If your credit card has been damaged or lost you can find out more on
canceling and replacing your card
here.
Once cancelled, your card will no longer be valid and you will be unable to pay bills via direct debit or make purchases using your card. You will need to contact any providers you pay using this credit card to arrange an alternative method of payment. Also, please be aware that any pending transactions may be charged to your credit card account after the card has been cancelled. You’ll still be liable to pay for these transactions and any related interest and fees associated with them.
To cancel your credit card, call our contact centre on
0800 255 382
or visit your nearest
ASB branch
.

question: If someone has died, can I access their money for a funeral & related expenses?
answer:
Yes you can. If you would like assistance covering the funeral and related expenses using funds from the deceased estate, a staff member in branch or the Contact Centre will be able to assist you. Funds can either be paid directly to the funeral director or to make a reimbursement to any person(s) who have already paid funeral and funeral related expenses.
An application form (Indemnity for release of funds to pay funeral expenses) for this can be found by clicking
here
. You will need to provide a certified true copy of the funeral invoice or receipt of any funeral or funeral related expenses and an original or certified copy of the death certificate. Please note that this is a sworn declaration and as such will need to be witnessed by a JP, Solicitor or Notary Public.
For further information, view our online
Deceased Estates brochure
.

question: What kind of deceased estates are there?
answer:
The deceased estates diagram featured
here
will be able to help you identify what kind of estate you are involved with.
For further information, view our online
Deceased Estates brochure
.

question: What should I do with a bank account following a death?
answer:
It is best for you to let ASB know of the death as soon as possible. Holds will be placed on the deceased’s accounts while the estate matters are arranged and ASB will help you through the next steps.
If the deceased’s personal accounts are held in joint names, the balance usually becomes the property of the surviving persons; in this case a hold will not need to be placed on the accounts and the deceased’s name will be removed from the account.
ASB can provide the proven executors, administrator or Next of Kin with a list of all products and on-going instructions held by us if requested. This includes automatic payment instructions, bank accounts, insurance products, investments and the ASB KiwiSaver scheme.
Accounts held solely in the deceased name will be made available once the closing documents have been received by ASB. The balance will be made available to the proven executors, administrator or Next of Kin of the estate.
For further information, view our online
Deceased Estates brochure
.

question: Is there a user guide I can download for FastPhone?
answer:
Yes. Go to
www.asb.co.nz/documents/banking-with-asb/fastphone-user-guide
and you’ll find all the information you need to get started including setting up your FastPhone, handy hints, useful keys and much more.

question: How many account suffixes can I open online?
answer:
You can open a maximum of 3 suffixes per customer per day online.
Please note:
If your account is overdrawn then you will not be able to open a new suffix.

question: Does it cost anything to open a Foreign Currency Account?
answer:
There are no charges to open a Foreign Currency account and there are no monthly account fees.
Find out more about
ASB Foreign Currency Accounts
.

question: Is there a limit on how much I can pay into my ASB account?
answer: There's no daily limits for deposits to your account if they are cleared funds. If the funds you're receiving are uncleared, we will automatically clear $5,000 of the total amount and the rest will be put on hold for 3 Business days before being automatically cleared. If these funds come into an interest bearing account they will still continue to earn interest even whilst funds are on hold.

question: How do I change the name on my ASB account?
answer:
If you have legally changed your name, you will need to visit one of our
ASB Branches
with either a Marriage certificate or Name Change certificate and also a Passport or Drivers Licence so we can validate and update your identification. Once we review these documents, we can change the name of your account.

question: Are there delays for accessing deposits into a newly opened ASB account?
answer: For the first 21 days after a new account has been opened there will be a 3 business day hold for every deposit.

question: Can I use Mx as my preferred title with ASB?
answer: Yes you can. Mx (pronounced Miks) is now an option should you prefer to use this as your formal title instead of Mr, Mrs, or Miss. Mx is used in the LGBTI community as a non-gender specific salutation and its introduction by ASB is a further reflection of our commitment to Diversity and Inclusion. If you would like to update your title to Mx, you can request this using FastNet My Messages, a private Message on Facebook, call our Contact Centre 0800 803 804 or pop into a branch.

question: How can I rename my accounts in FastNet Business?
answer:
When you first log into FastNet Business and view your accounts, besides your account number(s) the product name of your account will appear.  You can change the ‘known as’ name on your FastNet Business site to display a more meaningful name.  To do this:
- Go into Administration
- Click Client
- Click on Rename Accounts
This will produce the full list of accounts your FastNet Business site has access to. To change the name(s):
- Select your account
- Click Edit
- Enter in your preferred name into the ‘Account Known As’ field.
Note: Only an administrator will have the permissions to make these changes. Once made the known as name will appear for all FastNet Business users.

question: If a person has died, how do I know if I can manage the deceased account?
answer:
Who ASB may deal with about the bank accounts of a deceased customer will depend on whether the deceased person had prepared a will. If a will had been prepared then bank account matters can be dealt with by the executors named in the will.
If there was no will then a person is said to have died ‘intestate’. In this case, the Next of Kin may be required to apply to the High Court for appointment as administrator under a grant of Letters of Administration. The administrator is required to distribute the estate amongst close relatives according to fixed rules set out in the Administration Act. In this case, bank account matters can be dealt with by the administrator or, if Letters of Administration are not being sought, the Next of Kin.
For further information, view our online
Deceased Estates brochure
.

question: I'm using FastPhone, what’s my account suffix?
answer: It’s the last two digits of your account number.  FastPhone will often ask you for a suffix of the account you wish to access. A good idea is to keep a note of your different account suffixes on your wallet card.

question: How do I check a Direct Debit that I didn't authorise which has come from my account?
answer:
For questions about Direct Debits that you have authorised, you need to speak to the business or person you authorised to debit your account.
If your account is debited and you didn’t authorise a deduction, or you previously requested the Direct Debit be deleted; we can trace the authority to confirm the authorisation. You will need to speak to us about this by calling 0800 803 804 or by visiting any
ASB branch.
Deleting a Direct Debit Authority will stop further deductions from your account under that authority. However, deductions may begin again if you gave someone more than one authorisation, or you provided them with a new authorisation.

question: I've forgotten my FastPhone ID Code? What can I do?
answer:
You need to call our team on
0800 803 804,
or come into any ASB Branch and we’ll issue you a new one.
Can I change my ID Code at any time?
Yes, you can.  Here’s how:
Call FastPhone on
0800 272 272
and key in your Customer Access Number, your current ID code and press 6. Then follow the instructions.
What type of phone can I use for telephone banking?
Any touchtone telephone will work, including mobile phones.

question: What do all the numbers on my ASB Bank account mean, what are they used for?
answer:
Each of the numbers on your ASB account has a very good reason for being there. Let’s break it down and explain by segments.
BB-OOOO-XXXXXXX-SS
BB:
This is the bank you hold the account with eg 12 means your account is with ASB.
OOOO:
This number represents the branch where your account is held.
XXXXXXX:
This is your unique account number.
SS:
This is your account suffix. Different products have different suffixes to keep them separate from each other.  For example the suffix code range for savings accounts is 50 to 60.

question: How do I register for two-step verification using the ASB Mobile Banking app?
answer:
If you meet the eligibility criteria, you'll be able to complete two-step verification using the ASB Mobile app.
You can use your app to approve two-step verification requests for:
Logging in to FastNet Classic internet banking.
Making one-off payments in internet banking.
Verifying your identity on the phone.
To verify that you are eligible:
You should ensure you have the latest version of the ASB Mobile app to ensure compatibility with two-step verification. See
How do I update the ASB Mobile Banking app?
You must be registered for Netcode. See
How do I register for Netcode?
Your ASB Mobile app must be Netcode verified.
To check if your app is Netcode verified, follow these steps below:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Passwords and security’.
If your app is Netcode verified, it will show ‘ON’ next to ‘Netcode verified’.
If your app is not Netcode verified, it will show ‘OFF’. Tap on this to begin the Netcode verification process.
You should ensure that push notifications are enabled in your device settings. To check if your device has push notifications enabled, follow these steps below:
For iOS:
Open the ‘Settings’ app.
Scroll to ‘ASB Mobile’.
Select ‘Notifications’.
Ensure ‘Allow notifications’ is enabled.
For Android:
Open the ‘Settings’ app.
Select ‘Notifications’.
Select ‘ASB Mobile’.
Swipe right on the toggle bar to enable notifications.
You may be asked to complete two-step verification when we detect something unusual about your login attempt. If you would like to require two-step verification for all internet banking login attempts, see
how to enable Netcode at login
.

question: Do I have to update my Netcode if my phone number changes?
answer:
Yes, if you change your mobile number, then your Netcode will also have to be updated so that we can send it to your new number.
For your security and protection, you're not able do this online, but you can easily update it if you have the ASB Mobile Banking app.
Here’s how:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Select ‘Mobile numbers’ to update the number we send the Netcode verification to.
Tap ‘Save’.
Alternatively, you can call us on 0800 327 863, or if you're overseas +64 9 306 3000 between 8:00am and 8:00pm, or 8:30am and 5:00pm on weekends and public holidays NZ time. We'll ask you some security questions to establish you are you and update your details so you can receive Netcode on your new number.
Or visit any ASB branch, and the staff will be happy to help you.

question: I haven't received my notification for two-step verification using the ASB Mobile Banking app. What now?
answer:
First
, verify that you are eligible. See
How do I register for two-step verification using the ASB Mobile Banking app?
Next
, check that notifications are enabled in your device settings. If you need to enable notifications, see
How do I turn push notifications on or off for the ASB Mobile banking app?
Tip: If you have push notifications disabled, you can retrieve the latest two-step verification request by logging into your ASB Mobile Banking app and tapping on the bell icon any time before the request expires.
Finally
, ensure your device is connected to the internet. Internet is required to receive and approve two-step verification requests on ASB Mobile Banking app. You can try turning Wi-Fi on or off, then trying again.
If you don’t have internet on your mobile device, you can still choose to verify using a text message or physical token.

question: What is Netcode at login?
answer:
Netcode at login is a
two-step verification
option that helps make sure it’s really you when logging in to FastNet Classic internet banking. This extra layer of security helps protect your account from fraudulent activity and is free to use.
If you enable Netcode at login, you will be required to complete
two-step verification
by using the ASB Mobile Banking app, entering a Netcode sent to you by text message, or entering a Netcode from a Netcode token.
Here’s how you set it up:
Log in to FastNet Classic internet banking
Select your Profile in the top right-hand corner (click on your name)
Select Netcode at login and choose Yes
Click Submit
With Netcode at login turned on, you’ll still use your username and password to log in to FastNet Classic internet banking, but you’ll also be prompted to enter the Netcode sent to you in a TXT message or on your token. Netcode at login is not required when logging into the ASB Mobile Banking app.
Once logged in, you may be prompted for another Netcode for banking activity.
Learn more about Netcode.
How do I register for Netcode?
Please note: Netcode via SMS is free to use if you have a New Zealand mobile phone number, but please check with your provider in case there are charges to receive international text messages. Since we rely on international phone providers to send our Netcode SMS to international phone numbers, we cannot guarantee you’ll be able to receive Netcode SMS to your international phone number.

question: Can I use my Netcode token to register for ASB Mobile?
answer: Yes. Your Netcode tokens will be accepted during ASB Mobile app registration as well as Netcode SMS. If you are registered for both Netcode SMS and Netcode token, you can toggle between the two on the registration screen.

question: What is two-step verification?
answer:
Two-step verification is an extra layer of security that helps us make sure it’s really you when completing certain banking activities on your ASB account.
You may be asked to complete two-step verification when logging in, making larger payments, and certain other transactions using FastNet Classic.
You can complete two-step verification by using:
a notification that is sent to your ASB Mobile app,
a unique number texted to your Netcode registered mobile phone number, or
a unique number generated on a Netcode token that we provide to you.
Find out more about
two-step verification
.

question: What is a Netcode token?
answer:
A
Netcode
token is a small device from ASB that you can use in place of having Netcode texts sent to your mobile when doing your internet banking. It is very handy if you frequently lose service on your mobile, or are traveling overseas. Each token has an expiry date which can be found on the back of your token.
You can also choose to receive your Netcode via text message when using FastNet Classic internet banking. With this, you’ll receive your Netcode in a text message when a Netcode is required.
To request a Netcode token or register for Netcode via text message, call 0800 FASTNET (
0800 327 863
) and select option 2. If you're overseas you can call
+64 9 306 3000
.
We'll post your token out to you and when it arrives, just call 0800 FASTNET (
0800 327 863
) to activate it and you can start making Netcode authenticated transactions.

question: What happens if I did not receive the text message containing my OTP (One Time Pin) when setting up Google Pay?
answer: The One Time Pin (OTP) will be sent to your Netcode registered mobile number. If you did not receive your OTP it is likely that this mobile number is out of date. We can help you, just give the ASB Contact Centre a call or pop into your local branch to change your Netcode registered mobile number.

question: Do I need my Netcode token after I register for ASB Mobile Business?
answer: Yes, you will need your Netcode token when using FastNet Business, but not for authorising payments on ASB Mobile Business. Your mobile itself takes over from your Netcode token.

question: How do I order a Netcode Token for a User in FastNet Business?
answer:
All netcode tokens must be ordered via the FastNet Business Helpdesk.  You have a couple of options when requesting a Netcode token for a user
If a new token is required, you will need to complete a FastNet Business User Change request form
If you have a token from a previous user, you can reallocate this token to the new user. You will need to complete the FastNet Business User Change request form to request this

question: What do I do if my registered device has been lost or stolen?
answer:
You can deregister your lost and stolen device on another device with the ASB Mobile Banking app or FastNet Classic internet banking. Here’s how:
ASB Mobile app:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Settings’, and under ‘Devices’ select ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and tap 'Remove device'.
FastNet Classic:
Log in to your FastNet Classic account.
Click on 'Settings' in the top right of the page.
Click on ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and click 'Remove device'.
Click 'Remove' to confirm.
Tip: You can review the date a device was last used by selecting more information.
If you can’t access the ASB Mobile app or FastNet Classic, or need to block cards, call us on 0800 803 804, or +64 9 306 3000 if you are overseas to have your device deregistered.
Tip: You can also block any cards in cards control via the ASB Mobile Banking app.
We also have these useful guides to help you.
How to temporarily lock your ASB Visa Credit or Debit Card
How to cancel and replace your ASB Visa Credit or Debit card
ASB Card Control useful guides

question: Can I use my fingerprint / Face ID to log into the ASB Mobile Banking app?
answer:
Yes, you can log in to the ASB Mobile Banking app using your fingerprint / Touch ID or Face ID and access most in-app activities without entering your PIN or password. Please note there are a few activities that still require your PIN or password for further security, e.g. Forgot PIN.
Depending on the type of mobile device you have, there are different options that enable you to use Apple Touch ID / Face ID or Android Fingerprint Authorisation. This enables you to use biometric authorisation to log into ASB Mobile and replace the need for a PIN or password.
To enable Touch ID or Face ID log in for Apple devices:
Ensure Touch ID / Face ID is enabled on your device.
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Passwords and security’.
Select ‘Touch ID / ‘Face ID’.
Tap the selector to allow Touch ID / Face ID to log in.
Remember, we automatically disable Touch ID / Face ID for ASB Mobile, if the biometrics stored on your device are changed. You can turn Touch ID / Face ID back on in the ASB Mobile app by following the above steps.
To enable fingerprint log in for Android devices:
Ensure fingerprint is enabled on your device.
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Passwords and security’ and then select ‘Face and fingerprint recognition’.
Tap the selector to allow Face and fingerprint recognition’ to log in.
Remember, we automatically disable Fingerprint Login for ASB Mobile, if the biometrics stored on your device are changed. You can turn Fingerprint Login back on in the ASB Mobile app by following the above steps.

question: Where can I find my username?
answer:
You can find your username in you profile on the ASB Mobile Banking app, here’s how:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
You’ll see your username on the Profile screen.

question: What do I do if there’s a device registered for the ASB Mobile Banking app that I don’t recognise?
answer:
We take security extremely seriously. If you see a device, you do not recognise in your list of registered devices follow these steps:
Remove the device using either the ASB Mobile Banking app or FastNet Classic Internet Banking.
ASB Mobile app:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Settings’, and under ‘Devices’ select ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and tap 'Remove device'.
FastNet Classic:
Log in to your FastNet Classic account.
Click on 'Settings' in the top right of the page.
Click on ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and click 'Remove device'.
Click 'Remove' to confirm.
Tip: The last used by date and registration date can help you identify potentially suspicious activity.
If you think there has been fraudulent activity on your accounts:
Change your password
and review your accounts for any suspicious transactions or activity.
If you see any suspicious or unfamiliar activity and you think you have been targeted by an online or phone scam, let us know and we'll do everything we can to help. Call 0800 ASB FRAUD (0800 272 372), or +64 9 303 0332 if you're overseas.

question: What do I do if I no longer use a registered device?
answer:
Deregister your device once you have decided to sell or give away your device. You can deregister it on the same or another device with the ASB Mobile Banking app or FastNet Classic internet banking. Here’s how:
ASB Mobile app:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Settings’, and under ‘Devices’ select ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and tap 'Remove device'.
FastNet Classic:
Log in to your FastNet Classic account.
Click on 'Settings' in the top right of the page.
Click on ‘Manage devices’.
Select the device you wish to deregister and click 'Remove device'.
Tip: You can review the date a device was last used by selecting more information
If you can’t access the Mobile app or FastNet Classic, call us on 0800 803 804, or +64 9 306 3000 if you are overseas Mon - Fri 8:00am  8:00pm, or 8:30am and 5:00pm Sat, Sun & public holidays to have your device deregistered. Consider backing up your content on another device first.
Then before you sell (or give away) your phone or tablet restore it to its factory settings. That erases all your personal data.
Here's how:
Find the 'Reset' section of device’s settings menu.
Follow the prompts.
Or check on the relevant website:
iPhone
Samsung
Huawei
Oppo
HTC
Nokia

question: What is reCAPTCHA and why am I seeing the reCAPTCHA logo when I try to log into FastNet Classic internet banking or Online Share Trading?
answer:
reCAPTCHA is a commonly used Google based verification tool. It protects ASB's online website from automated malicious software, while letting real customers pass through with ease.
Occasionally, invisible reCAPTCHA will verify you as part of the FastNet Classic and / or Online Share Trading login process. We continuously review our security and introduced the reCAPTCHA process as part of this.
Find out more information on
reCAPTCHA

Google’s Privacy Policy
and
Terms of Use
.

question: Why has my high-risk transaction been declined?
answer:
To prevent potential financial loss to our customers, our fraud detection systems identify certain types of transactions that have a high likelihood of being fraudulent. These are identified as high risk due to other customers experiencing high levels of fraud with these types of transactions.
Can you still use your card?
Yes, your card will remain active, and you can continue to use it for everyday purchases. However, you will not be able to make this particular transaction using your ASB card. You will need to consider alternative options for payment should you wish to proceed with this transaction.
If you didn't attempt this transaction, we recommend you cancel and replace your card using the ASB Mobile Banking app. There is no cost to replacing your card, simply select 'Other' as the reason you are replacing your card within the Mobile app.
Learn how to do this by following our
step-by-step guide
.
For more information on how to keep yourself safe online, visit
asb.co.nz/security
.
Tools & Tips
When making online purchases, make sure you do your research on the purchase or business you are buying from. This could include doing third party reviews to check they are legitimate.
Be wary of unrealistic promises such as investments offering huge returns or out of the blue prizes.
Download the ASB Mobile Banking app and enable push notifications so that you receive important security alerts.
Use ASB Card Control on the ASB Mobile Banking app to manage your card(s) anywhere, anytime. Card Control allows you to temporarily lock your card if you lose it, turn off ATM withdrawals, set spending limits, request a replacement card and much more.
Finally it is important to remember that ASB will never send an email or text with links to login to our online banking.
If you remain concerned about the safety of your card(s) or your account(s), please reach out on 0800 ASB FRAUD (0800 272 372) or +64 9 306 3000 if you're overseas.

question: How do I create a strong password?
answer:
Strong passwords are long, random, easy for you to remember but hard for others to guess. There are a few tips and tricks to make a password that’s even stronger than minimum requirements.
Use a different password every time
It’s the first rule of password safety, but one of the hardest to implement. We have some tips on making it more memorable below!
Create a passphrase
A long password is a strong password. An easy way to make a long password that you can remember is to create a passphrase out of four or more random words. Because of their length, passphrases can be just as strong as a mix of numbers, letters and symbols.
Think
Cats-Milkshake-Sunny-Banana
or
Elephant_Jumping_Fence_Bicycle
.
Keep it random
Avoid using names, dates of birth or addresses, whether they're yours, a family member's or your pet. They're easy to find out, and hackers can use them to guess your password.
Find it hard to remember your passwords?
Some people use password managers to remember their passwords for them. There are lots of options out there and using them isn’t a breach of our T&Cs, but make sure to do your research and check they are reputable and have excellent security credentials.
Remember: The best passwords are easy for you to remember, but hard for others to guess.

question: What is Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and how could it affect me?
answer:
Money laundering is all about hiding the profits of crime. Criminals try lots of ways to make the money they make from illegal activities appear legitimate.
Those who finance terrorism use similar methods to hide their tracks.
In 2013 New Zealand introduced identification provisions of the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism Act 2009. It requires all financial institutions to detect and stop money laundering and terrorism financing. It helps protect all of us from financial crime and maintain our country’s international reputation.
One of its requirements is for us to carefully verify every customer’s identity, using photo ID and proof of address.
We usually only need this when you open an account. Sometimes, we need to update your details for other reasons:
When you want to add someone else as a signing authority on your account
If your account activity changes over time
When you deposit cash into another person’s account
When a non-customer deposits cash into your account
When you send, receive or transfer funds

question: I'm in Online Share Trading, but my sell order was not accepted. Why was this?
answer:
Some of the reasons your order is not accepted might be:
Your specified price is too far away from the current market price.
The security has been suspended/halted or delisted.
Your Common Shareholder Number (CSN) or standard holder number is invalid.
Your FIN is invalid.
The stock is registered as being owned by another person(s).
You have insufficient units available for settlement in either your holding with the relevant share registry for New Zealand Stock Exchange (NZX) listed securities.
Or, specifically for Australian securities on the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX):
You have insufficient units available in your CHESS holdings sponsored by ASB Securities/Finclear Execution Ltd for ASX securities.
The stock is sponsored in CHESS by a broker other than ASB Securities/Finclear Execution Ltd.
The stock is issuer sponsored. If this is the case, you must contact us to place the order by telephone we may ask you to supply a copy of the issuer sponsored holding statement.
Find out more information on the
markets you can trade through ASB Securities
with our guide.

question: What options do I have if I want to buy a residential investment property and I don't have the required deposit?
answer:
Due to the Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s (RBNZ’s) loan-to-value ratio (LVR) restrictions, loans for residential investment properties generally have minimum equity requirements.
Find out more information about the
loan-to-value ratio (LVR)
.
If you have less than the deposit required and want to buy a residential investment property, there are a few things you can do:
Combining any other properties (such as residential non-investment property) you hold as part of the loan application which may have the effect of lowering the LVR, including obtaining an updated valuation on one or more of the properties
Subject to certain conditions being met, an exemption from the RBNZ restrictions may apply, such as construction lending or if you are refinancing from another bank
Our lending specialists can talk to you about these options. Simply call
0800 100 600
or visit your nearest
ASB branch
.

question: How do the new Reserve Bank of New Zealand (RBNZ) rules applying to property investment affect me?
answer:
Due to the Reserve Bank of New Zealand (RBNZ) Loan to Value Ratio (LVR) 'speed limit' restrictions, loans for residential investment properties generally have minimum equity requirements. Further information can be found
here
.
Exemptions may apply. When a loan falls under an exemption, the loan is not included in the banks high LVR 'speed limits'. If you would like to find out more about these exemptions and the restrictions applying to residential investment properties, talk to one of our Lending Specialists on 0800 100 600 or visit your nearest
ASB branch
.

question: What's the difference between locking and cancelling my EFTPOS card?
answer:
Locking your card is temporary. For example, you have misplaced your card or accidentally left it somewhere. You can unlock and continue to use the card once you have it back.
Cancelling your card is permanent. Once it's cancelled you’ll need to be issued a new card.
How do I lock or unlock my card?

question: How do I lock or unlock my EFTPOS card?
answer:
If your EFTPOS card has been lost, stolen or damaged, you can temporarily get it locked by calling us on
0800 803 804
or visiting your nearest
ASB branch
. You can contact us in the same way to unlock your card.
If you think it's gone for good, call us or visit your nearest ASB branch to cancel and replace your EFTPOS card.

question: What is a Card Security Code or CVV2 number?
answer: The Card Security Code or CVV2 number is the three digit code on the back of your ASB Visa credit card, at the end of the signature panel.

question: How safe and secure are ASB Securities Online Share Trading services?
answer:
ASB's online security policy is to take every practical step possible to keep your accounts secure. Like our other services, ASB Securities Online Share Trading has many layers of security; one of which is strong encryption, which scrambles information passing between you and ASB before it travels over the Internet.
To log in to ASB Securities Online Share Trading, you'll need to provide your unique username and password. You can also choose to enable Netcode at log in for an extra layer of security.
Find out how to register for Netcode
ASB Securities Online Share Trading also hold and Extended Validation (EV) SSL Certificate. It's a badge of authentication and means that if you're using a modern browser, your address bar will show a padlock next to its URL when you visit our site. This makes it easy for you to be sure you're on the genuine ASB website.
An important part of staying safe online means taking your own precautions. Safeguard your personal and account information and keep your computer and mobile devices updated with the latest security software and security patches.
It's important to keep your Access Code and Password to yourself. Regularly changing passwords is another way to keep your accounts secure. With the ASB Mobile banking app you can change your password easily - even if you've forgotten your old one.
Find out more information on
Online Share Trading
.

question: Why has the log in section moved for FastNet Business?
answer:
We have recently made changes to the way customers log into ASB FastNet Classic, internet banking for personal customers.
As a result, we changed the position of the FastNet Business log in section on our website.

question: Why does the new login ask for a Client ID?

Some customers have reported problems with the Log In panel at the top right of asb.co.nz.  In some cases the Log In panel shows three boxes for a Client ID, User ID and Password. question: What is a step-up security?
answer:
A step-up security is a hybrid debt instrument (a combination of debt and equity) that pays a fixed interest rate or margin up until a specified date (the step-up date).
Step-up securities are very similar to capital notes in that the issuer is looking for the rating agencies to accept them as equity when considering the rest of the company's debt. In order to get this equity credit from the rating agencies, the step-up securities are generally subordinated (meaning they rank below other debt), non-cumulative, unsecured notes.
Beyond the specified date, if the security has not yet been redeemed by the issuer, the distribution payment may step-up to a higher rate (this step-up rate is fixed prior to the issue of the security). A single security can have more than one step-up date during the life of that security. The terms for this will be clearly set out in the Investment Statement for the Initial Public Offering.

question: I’m looking at a business loan, can I use my house as security?
answer: In some circumstances, and depending on the type of loan, then that might be an option.  You’ll need to show you have sufficient equity in your home to support the business loan, and we’ll need to see your business plan.

question: Can I use my parent’s house as security or use a guarantor for my home loan?
answer:
It’s possible to use another property as security to purchase your home. The key to us making a lending decision will be making sure you can afford to make the repayments and considering the
Loan to Value Ratio (LVR
).
Using KiwiSaver and your savings to buy your first home may be helpful to meet lending criteria. If you would like to chat to a Lending Specialist about your options, simply call 0800 100 600 or visit your nearest
ASB branch.

question: Why has my KiwiSaver or investment balance dropped so much?
answer:
It can be unsettling if you see your balance drop on your KiwiSaver or other investment accounts. These accounts are invested in financial markets, so values will naturally go up and down when global news and events unfold. This is a normal part of investing.
Learn more about market volatility and ASB’s approach to market conditions
.

question: How much money do I need to invest in order to meet with a Wealth Manager?

question: What is the annual government contribution and when will it be added to my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account?
answer:
If you are contributing to your KiwiSaver account, you may be eligible to receive an annual government contribution. The annual government contribution is calculated each year based on your contributions from 1 July to 30 June. The Government will contribute 25 cents for each dollar you contribute, to a maximum of $260.72. To receive the maximum, you'll need to contribute at least $1,042.86. You can achieve this by contributing around $21 each week.
To be eligible, you must be a member of a KiwiSaver scheme, 16 years or older, not yet eligible for retirement withdrawals, and be living mainly in New Zealand. If you don’t meet these requirements for the full year (1 July to 30 June), the maximum you can receive will be based on the part of the year you were eligible, as well as the contributions you make.
KiwiSaver members who have a taxable income of over $180,000 per year will not be eligible for the annual government contribution. Please see the
Inland Revenue website
for more details.
If you have earned a salary of $34,762 and contributed at least 3% to your KiwiSaver account over the course of the year, then you should receive the maximum annual government contribution.
If you are eligible for the annual government contribution, we’ll claim it on your behalf and you will see it in your ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account shortly after the 30th of June each year.
Please note, employer contributions, previous government contributions and amounts transferred from Australian complying superannuation funds do not count towards eligibility for annual government contributions.
Find out how to
maximise your government contributions
.

question: How does KiwiSaver impact employees under 18?
answer:
Employees under 18 must enrol directly with a KiwiSaver provider. While you don’t need to make employer contributions
*
, you should deduct employee contributions at a minimum of 3% of their before-tax salary or wages.
Under 16s aren’t eligible for the annual government contribution.
*
From 1 April 2026, you must start making employer contributions for those aged 16 and older with the minimum employee and employer contribution rates increasing from 3% to 3.5% from this date.
Members will be able to temporarily opt down to the current 3% rate, if they choose, and still be matched at that rate by you.

question: Am I eligible for the KiwiSaver annual government contribution?
answer:
You will be eligible for the
annual government contribution
if you:
are a member of a KiwiSaver scheme,
are 16 or older,
are not yet eligible to make
retirement withdrawals
,
reside mainly in New Zealand (or you’re a state services employee working outside New Zealand or work overseas for some charities)
contributed to your KiwiSaver account during the year, and
have a taxable income less than $180,000 each year
*
The Government will contribute 25 cents for every dollar you contribute (up to a maximum of $260.72 each year).
You may not receive the full amount if you don’t meet these requirements for the full year (1 July to 30 June).
*
KiwiSaver members who have a taxable income of over $180,000 per year will not be eligible for the annual government contribution. Please see the
Inland Revenue website
for more details.

question: I’m enrolled in the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme, can I still get the annual government contribution if I’m on a savings suspension?
answer:
Yes. If you are on a savings suspension, you can still get the annual government contribution if you're eligible; however you'll need to make voluntary contributions of at least $1,042.86 during the year to get the maximum government contribution of $260.72. Making voluntary payments won’t affect your savings suspension.
KiwiSaver members who have a taxable income of over $180,000 per year will not be eligible for the annual government contribution. Please see the
Inland Revenue website
for more details.

question: Market volatility and investments
answer:
What does Market Volatility Mean?
Market Volatility refers to ups and downs in investment values. From time to time, we will see periods of heightened movements in value, usually linked to economic or other market events. What this typically means for things like your KiwiSaver savings or other investment products (like Investment Funds and direct shares), is that day to day balances or values can move up or down more than usual. As a result, this can put pressure on the value of your investments in the short-term. It’s important to note that volatility is a natural characteristic of investing in financial markets.
What effect can Market Volatility have on my investments?
When you invest in a managed fund like KiwiSaver or Investment Funds, your contributions are used to buy “units” in your selected fund. These units represent your share of the fund, which made up of a mix of assets - Growth Assets & Income Assets. The number of units you own does not reduce during periods of volatility, however it can impact the value of these units, moving them up and down. Think about this the same way you would by owning a share in a property - the value of the property can go up and down, however your share remains the same.
What is the best way to manage Market Volatility?
There’s no need to panic
. Seeing your balance fluctuate can create understandable concern. Market downturns can happen and sometimes they can be big, but these ups and downs are a normal part of investing.
Stay focused on your long-term goals,
check back in with your goal or get advice from an expert. Your investment goals, timeframe, and appetite for investment risk help decide the best fund option for you, and it’s important not to react to short-term events. History shows us that over time, share markets recover from temporary falls.
During the initial Covid-induced volatility period, around Feb-March 2020, a lot of investors moved their KiwiSaver accounts to lower risk funds. While this may have made them feel more comfortable initially, research from the
FMA
(Financial Markets Authority) suggests that this may have had a detrimental long-term effect on their savings. And as we know, markets recovered those losses and then proceeded to add significant gains.
Contribute regularly,
if you’re adding to your investments regularly, then temporary dips can actually be good news. Continuing to add money to your savings while the market dips, could have a positive impact on your overall investment. This is because you are buying units at a lower price, so every dollar is buying more units in your fund than usual. Over time as the market recovers, these lower priced units can potentially improve your overall balance (or the value of the units you hold).
Make sure you’re in the right fund,
from time to time your goals, financial situation, timeframe or attitude towards risk may change– if so, or if it’s been a while since you last checked in, periods like this are another reason to seek
advice
, to ensure you’re in the right fund, to reach your savings goal.
Stay up to date with information,
we have a range of
Economic Insights
available to stay up to date with conditions likely to influence markets around the world.
Where can I get guidance and advice on my KiwiSaver account?
Advice
If you have any questions about your KiwiSaver account, the easiest way to talk to one of our KiwiSaver Specialists is on
0800 272 738
. They’re available from 8am to 8pm during weekdays, or 8:30am to 5pm on Saturdays. While you’re on the phone it’s a great chance to check that you’re making the most of your KiwiSaver account. We can take you through our KiwiSaver advice tool to check you’re on track - it only takes around 15 minutes.
Tools
If you’d prefer to do it yourself, as an ASB customer you can get digital advice through our
KiwiSaver advice tool
to find out which fund is right for you. We’ll take you through a series of simple questions and provide a recommendation on your options to make sure you’re maximising your savings towards your first home or retirement. You can complete this digital advice tool on our website, through FastNet Classic internet banking or the ASB Mobile banking app.
We also have other simple and easy digital tools for anyone to use at home. Use our
KiwiSaver Calculator
to revisit your long term savings projection and goal, or our
Help me Choose
tool to be reminded of your fund choice and why it’s right for you.
Helpful resources
We have a range of useful web resources available. Our
KiwiSaver Investor Hub
webpage provides up to date market commentary, blogs,
Economic Insights
and the latest news impacting your investment in the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme.
If you’d prefer to listen in, we have over 50 episodes of the
ASB Investment Podcast
to learn more about current market events.

question: What’s the minimum amount I can invest in ASB Investment Funds?
answer:
There is no minimum investment amount to get started with ASB Investment Funds. Once your account is open you can make additional investments of any amount by:
A one-off lump sum
A regular direct debit
You can make lump sum investments through ASB FastNet Classic, the ASB Mobile Banking app or by contacting the ASB Wealth Advisory Service on 0800 108 084.
If you apply for ASB Investment Funds using ASB Fastnet Classic or the ASB Mobile Banking app, you can set up a regular direct debit at the same time. To set up a regular direct debit at a later date, you'll need to contact the ASB Wealth Advisory Service on 0800 108 084.
If we introduce minimum investment or direct debit amounts in the future, we will let you know.

question: Can I add more money to my term deposit or ASB Term Fund account?
answer:
No, once a term deposit or ASB Term Fund account is set up you won’t be able to add more money to your investment.
However, once your investment matures you will have a limited time to add funds to the current investment. You can do this at the same time you select a new term and rate when you
reinvest your term deposit
or ASB Term Fund.
If you do have extra money to invest, you could open another term investment or a high-interest savings account. You can do this through
FastNet Classic internet banking
- just go to 'Open and apply' in the menu.

question: What’s the difference between a term deposit and an ASB Term Fund account?
answer:
There are two key differences between a term deposit and an ASB Term Fund account:
How the investment is taxed, and
When you can access your money
At a glance:
Tax rate
When money can be accessed
Term deposit
Your income tax rate
At maturity or with 31 days’ notice. Earlier access is also possible in exceptional circumstances approved by ASB.
ASB Term Fund
Your Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR)
At maturity only. Early access is only possible in exceptional circumstances approved by ASB.
How the investment is taxed:
With a term deposit, your interest is taxed at your income tax rate.
With an ASB Term Fund, your share of income earned by the ASB Term Fund is taxed at your
Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR)
of 0%, 10.5%, 17.5% or 28%.
So, if your income tax rate is more than 28% during the term of your deposit, you could get a better after-tax return by investing in an ASB Term Fund compared to the after-tax return of a term deposit offering the same rate for the same term and charging the same fees.
You can work out your
PIR using our PIR calculator
,
or visit Inland Revenue’s website for more information on
PIRs
.
When you can access your money:
With a term deposit, you can access your money before the maturity date either by giving us 31 days’ notice or applying for an early withdrawal under
ASB’s exceptional circumstance application process
. A reduced interest rate will be applied to the amount withdrawn early.
With an ASB Term Fund, you won’t be able to access your money until maturity date. Early access before maturity date is only possible in exceptional circumstances approved by ASB. A break fee will be charged for early withdrawals from your account.

question: How can I get my money out of ASB Investment Funds?
answer:
Call us on 0800 803 804 or email
invest@asb.co.nz
to request a withdrawal from your ASB Investment Funds account.
You can choose the following withdrawal options:
Withdraw one-off lump sum amounts of at least $500 each time.
Set up automatic withdrawal amounts of at least $100 every fortnight or every month.
Transfer all or part of your investment to someone else.
If any of these change, we’ll let you know. If the value of your investment is below $100 for more than one year, we can withdraw your investment on your behalf and pay it to you. We can delay or suspend a withdrawal or transfer of your investment in exceptional circumstances in accordance with the Trust Deed. You will not receive any other distributions from the funds.

question: Can I receive interest monthly on a term deposit or ASB Term Fund?
answer:
Yes, if your investment in a term deposit or an ASB Term Fund account is for a term of six months or more and has a balance of $10,000 or more.
The compound interest option isn't available when interest is paid monthly.

question: Do I still qualify for employer contributions after I turn 65 and I’m still working?
answer:
Your employer doesn’t have to make contributions after you reach 65. If you’re still working and making employee contributions, talk to your employer to see if they might continue your employer contributions as well.
Check out our
KiwiSaver for Over 65s
page for more information.

question: Can I make a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal to purchase land?
answer: Yes, a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal can be used to buy land, provided you intend to build a home to live in on that land. Make sure you apply before you buy, as once you own the land, you won’t be eligible for a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal.

question: When can I get my KiwiSaver savings for my retirement?
answer:
You can withdraw your savings when you turn 65. If you have any questions about retirement withdrawal eligibility give us a call on 0800 ASB RETIRE (
0800 272 738
).
Check out our
KiwiSaver for Over 65s
page for more information.

question: Who can I contact to get assistance with Wealth Central?
answer:
For assistance or more information visit the
Wealth Central
page.

question: How do I use my Online Share Trading Portfolio to monitor the performance of my shares?
answer:
Log In to Online Share Trading and select Portfolio in the left hand menu. If you have not set up a portfolio, your Automated Portfolio will appear.
Any shares that you have purchased through Online Share Trading will show in your Automated Portfolio.
To create a personalised portfolio, beside the heading Automated Portfolio, select the + symbol and then enter a name for your new portfolio.
You can choose whether you would like your new portfolio to be your default by checking the box.
Once you select Done, your new portfolio will appear.
If you would like your portfolio to automatically update with any trades you do through Online Share Trading, check the Auto tick box.
You can also choose to include brokerage and fees in your portfolio calculations by checking the Include Bro/Fees tick box.
Find out
how to use your Portfolio in Online Share Trading
in our guide.

question: Do I need to take my money out of the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme when I become eligible for retirement?
answer:
You can leave your savings invested and continue to make contributions to your ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account after you are eligible for retirement withdrawals. You can also choose to withdraw some of it, all of it or set up a regular withdrawal.
Check out our
KiwiSaver for Over 65s
page for more information.

question: How do I reinvest my ASB Term Investment?
answer:
If your term investment has matured and you're within five days of maturity, you can use either FastNet Classic internet banking or the ASB Mobile Banking app to make changes to your investment:
To reinvest using
FastNet Classic
:
Log into FastNet Classic.
On the Balances page, select the term investment you want to reinvest.
Follow the simple steps to reinvest and select new
terms and rates
.
Or use the
ASB Mobile banking app
:
Log into the ASB Mobile app.
On the Accounts page, select the term investment you want to reinvest.
Follow the simple steps to reinvest and select new
terms and rates
.
You can also visit our helpful How to guide that will step you through the process. Further information can be found
here
.

question: Where can I find out more information about KiwiSaver?
answer:
For general information about KiwiSaver, check:
www.ird.govt.nz/kiwisaver
(for information about KiwiSaver)
www.sorted.org.nz
(for information about saving and budgeting)
You can find more information about the
ASB KiwiSaver Scheme
here.

question: If I use my KiwiSaver first home withdrawal, how long do I have to live in the property?
answer: There's no minimum time you must live in a house bought using a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal.

question: Can I apply for a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal if my partner has previously owned a home but I haven’t?
answer: Yes – each KiwiSaver first home withdrawal application is assessed on an individual basis.

question: What is a Shareholder Reference Number (SRN)?
answer:
A Shareholder Reference Number (SRN) is used to identify your Issuer Sponsored Shares listed on Australian Stock Exchange (ASX). Issuer Sponsored Shares are maintained by the issuing company – usually through an appointed share registry (not through the
CHESS system
, which is the standard broker holding subregister system of the ASX).
You’ll have a different SRN for each company you own shares in, and you’ll need your SRN to sell those shares. Your SRN can be found on your shareholding statement or other communications from the share registry and will be pre-fixed with the character I.
To sell your Issuer Sponsored Shares, please call us on
0800 272 732
with your SRN and ASB Securities client number.
We will need to check that the names and addresses of both your CHESS account and shareholdings match to ensure your trades can go ahead.  We can then load it before you start trading.
Find out more with our guide to
placing your first online order
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: How long does it take to process an ASB KiwiSaver Scheme full withdrawal/account closure?
answer:
Around 8 to 10 working days from when we get your application. The process takes longer than you might expect because your final
KiwiSaver Government contributions
(also known as a member tax credit) may need to be claimed from the Inland Revenue.

question: I've been sent a PIE Tax Certificate for my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account, what is it for?
answer:
Your PIE (Prescibed Interest Entities) tax certificate is sent to you every year, usually in May, and details the tax payable (or refundable) on your ASB KiwiSaver Scheme investment for the year to 31 March (tax year). It also includes the
Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR)
we held for you for the tax year.
Find out more information about what PIR you should use
and inform us of any change.
As long as the PIR you nominated for the tax year was correct, you won't need to pay any additional tax in respect of your interest in the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme. For more details see the
ASB KiwiSaver Scheme PIE Tax Certificate guide
.
For more information about how your investment is taxed see section 6 (What taxes will you pay?) of the
Other Material Information
document.

question: Can I join the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme?
answer:
You can join the
ASB KiwiSaver Scheme
if you:
are a New Zealand citizen or entitled to live in New Zealand indefinitely and
live, or normally live, in New Zealand (or you're a state services employee working outside New Zealand)
You are also eligible to transfer to the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme if you are already a member of another KiwiSaver scheme.

question: What happens to KiwiSaver contributions if I have employees with more than one job?
answer:
After they join
KiwiSaver
, you should deduct contributions from their next payday. If an employee (who is over 18 and not yet eligible to make retirement withdrawals) is making KiwiSaver contributions, you need to make employer contributions too.

question: Information on the change in Australian trading agent from Commsec to FinClear.
answer:
Will I be able to place, amend or cancel orders online during the weekend of the migration from Friday 19 November 2021 through to Monday 22 November 2021?
Unfortunately not, our online platform will be unavailable from 6.15pm Friday 19 November until 10am Monday 22 November.  ASB Securities staff will be available from 7am on Monday 22 November to assist you with any order related queries.
What happens to my open orders when you migrate from Commsec to FinClear?
All open orders will be cancelled from the market when we transition to Finclear, and be reinstated, unfortunately you will lose any existing priority in the queue. Orders may not be reinstated and remain cancelled if your specified price is deemed too far away from the market price by Finclear. You will be able to view the status of your order on our online platform on the “View My Trades” page.
What happens to my existing holdings with Commsec as my sponsoring broker?
Your holdings will remain on your current HIN, your new sponsoring broker will become FinClear.
Why are you moving from Commsec?
CommSec have reviewed their business model and are no longer looking to offer third party trading, which means that ASB Securities had to find a new trading agent and has opted to utilise FinClear’s third party trading services.
Will we see any new features coming with the move away from Commsec?
ASB Securities current focus is on a successful migration of our customers and there is potential for review in future, all existing functionality will remain the same i.e. we will not remove any functionality from you.
Will my holdings be safe with FinClear?
Yes, your holdings will remain on your current HIN, your new sponsoring broker will become FinClear.
I haven’t heard of FinClear before, who are they?
FinClear are a reputable ASX trading participant and other NZ brokers utilise their trading services, for more information you can visit their website at
www.finclear.com.au
.
Also, important to note ASB have a robust procurement process and assessed other ASX trading participants and concluded FinClear were superior in their offering.
Do I need to do anything?
No action required on your part if you are happy to transition to Finclear; everything remains the same aside from the change in sponsoring broker.
Will my existing dividend payments be affected?
No change, the registries will continue to distribute in the same manner as now.
Will my HIN change?
No, your HIN will remain the same.
I want to stay with Commsec, what do I need to do?
You can sign up directly with Commsec via their
website
, but you will not be able to trade on the NZX via Commsec.
Can I trade directly with Finclear, why go through yourselves as the middle-man?
No, you cannot trade directly, FinClear are not a retail broker.
I don’t wish to trade on the Australian market anymore, how do I close my Australian trading account?
Please email
asbsecinfo@asb.co.nz
with your client number and request and we will telephone you to verify your instruction and proceed to close the Australian account.
I have existing Australian holdings and don’t want to move to FinClear, what are my options?
You can either establish a relationship with another broker who offers access to the Australian market and they would then assist moving your shares via a broker to broker transfer, or you can request to have your holdings to be released and become Issuer Sponsored with the share registry.
I no longer require an ASB Securities share trading account and would like to close the entire facility.
Please download the
account closure form
our website, complete and return to
asbsecinfo@asb.co.nz
.

question: What is a Portfolio Investment Entity (PIE)?
answer:
A PIE is a type of investment entity that attributes taxable income to its investors and pays tax on their behalf at their Prescribed Investment Rate (PIR). The ASB KiwiSaver Scheme is a PIE.
You can find out more about PIRs by visiting our
PIR help page
. If you need to update your PIR, you can do so in FastNet Classic internet banking:
Log in and select your Profile in the top right hand corner.
Select Update Tax Details.
You’ll see your tax details we have for you.
Make any changes you need to.
Select Save.
Or by
contacting us
.
For more information about how your KiwiSaver investment is taxed see section 6 (What taxes will you pay?) of the
Other Material Information
document.

question: What do I do if my Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR) changes?
answer:
You can find out more about PIRs by visiting our
PIR help page
. If you need to update your PIR, you can do so in FastNet Classic internet banking or by
contacting us
.
To update in FastNet Classic:
Log in and select your Profile in the top right hand corner.
Select Update Tax Details.
You’ll see your tax details we have for you.
Make any changes you need to.
Select Save.
If your PIR was recorded incorrectly for a prior year, and you were on a higher PIR but should have been on a lower rate, you will not be able to claim back any of the extra tax you have paid. If you were on a lower PIR than you should have been, you will need to include this income in your tax return and Inland Revenue can assess the tax on this income at your personal tax rate.

question: I'm thinking about trading through ASB Securities, what is CHESS?
answer:
CHESS is a computerised share sub-register system operated by the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX), used to manage share ownership and settle trades on the ASX. As an ASB Securities share trading client, you will automatically have a CHESS account opened for you, which is sponsored by Finclear Execution Ltd, enabling you to trade on the ASX. Having your shares CHESS sponsored has a number of advantages:
You can trade or sell your shares immediately. Alternatively with issuer sponsorship, you cannot sell your shares unless you know your Shareholder Reference Number (SRN). It can take up to six weeks to get your SRN from some companies.
You have only one Holder Identification Number (HIN) for all your shareholdings. Alternatively with issuer-sponsored shares, you are given a different SRN for each company. A single HIN is easier to remember and, if you change address, you only need to notify us rather than notifying every company that you hold shares in.
You elect to have Finclear Execution Ltd sponsor you into CHESS by filling in the section in the Client Service Agreement when you register as a client.
If you already have issuer-sponsored shares or any shares sponsored by another sharebroker, you can arrange to have them transferred to Finclear Execution Ltd by emailing your instructions to us including copies of any holding statements.
Find out more about
buying and selling shares on online share trading
.

question: What is a default KiwiSaver scheme?
answer:
If you don’t choose your own KiwiSaver scheme and your employer doesn’t have a preferred scheme, then you will be allocated to a KiwiSaver scheme by Inland Revenue. That scheme is known as a default KiwiSaver scheme.
There are a number of default KiwiSaver scheme providers. They were selected by the Government based on a range of factors, including investment experience, fees, member education and organisation capabilities.

question: I'm in Online Share Trading, and I'm trying to amend/cancel my order but I can't. Why won't this work?
answer:
In Online Share Trading, when you’ve clicked ‘Amend/cancel order’ under My Menu, ensure you check the tick box to the left of the order you would like to cancel or amend.
The options to ‘Cancel order’ and ‘Amend order’ will be highlighted in blue for you to select (these are greyed out if you have not checked the tick box).
If you do not receive confirmation of your amendment/cancellation request please try the following:
Disabling any ad-block software as that can lead to buttons not displaying.
Clearing your cookies and cache and restarting your browser.
Update your browser to the latest version.
Use an alternative browser.
After trying the above, we recommend you log out of your session and restart your browser to ensure settings are updated.
If you still have trouble, please call us on 0800 272 732 weekdays 7am-6pm.
For more information see our guide on
how to buy and sell shares in Online Share Trading
.

question: Can I use my KiwiSaver savings to buy a house in the name of a trust?
answer: Yes, provided the trust is purchasing a property that is your first home, you are both a trustee and beneficiary of the trust and you intend to live in the property as your principal place of residence. To be eligible, your name (as the KiwiSaver member applying for a first home withdrawal) must be on the sale and purchase agreement (or on a deed of nomination).

question: Can I get my money out of the investments a Wealth Manager recommends?
answer:
Yes, subject to the recommended investment product or service criteria. Please refer to the ASB Investment Funds Product Disclosure Statement and the ASB Portfolio Series Service Disclosure Statement for more information.
You can obtain a copy of the Product Disclosure Statement for ASB Investment Funds and the ASB Portfolio Series Service Disclosure Statement by calling 0800 ASB FUNDS (
0800 272 386
) or by emailing
invest@asb.co.nz
.

question: Can I apply for a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal if my partner has previously owned a home but I haven’t?
answer:
Yes - each KiwiSaver first home withdrawal application is assessed on an individual basis.
Note, if you’re applying to
Kāinga Ora
for a First Home Grant they'll assess the application based on all purchasers of the property meeting the criteria.

question: Can I apply for a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal if I’ve owned a home before?
answer:
If you've owned property before but Kāinga Ora assesses you to be in a similar financial position to a first home buyer, you may be eligible for a first home withdrawal.
Find out more at the
Kāinga Ora website
Email
firsthome.enquiries@kaingaora.govt.nz
Or call
0508 935 266
.
If you’re eligible, Kāinga Ora will give you an approval letter which you must include with your application.

question: What happens when an employee who already has a KiwiSaver scheme starts work?
answer: If they’re not on a savings suspension, start deducting employee contributions from their first payday. If an employee (who is over 18 and not yet eligible to make retirement withdrawals) is making KiwiSaver contributions, you need to make employer contributions too.

question: Why am I seeing a property in Wealth Central that shouldn't be there?
answer:
The property is noted as a security held by ASB. If this is incorrect, please call us on
0800 100 600
weekdays 8:00am - 8:00pm or Saturday 9:00am - 5:00pm to let us know what has changed and why. We will advise our lending operations to discharge this property.

question: From which date can I see my investment history data?
answer: Your investment history data will show for the last 12 months.

question: Why am I missing an investment in the investment history graph?
answer:
Your investment history graph shows all unhidden, open accounts that have more than 2 months’ history and a balance that is not equal to zero. If you have hidden an investment and now want it to be included, go to 'Review your investments' in Wealth Central and select 'Hide/include investments'
.

question: What does the investment history graph in Wealth Central show?
answer:
The graph represents a trend of your monthly investment balances as at the 1st of each month and displays a maximum of 12 months of history. It excludes any closed or hidden investments.
The white dot represents the total of your selected savings and investments as of today.

question: Why is my investment value in the graph fluctuating over time?
answer:
Reasons for fluctuations are varied and can impact any of your investments. Some common reasons are:
Market changes
Deposits into or withdrawals from an investment
Accrued interest
Closing an account
Property loan value changes

question: Why does my non-ASB investment not show under the right category?
answer: This feature is currently not available for non-ASB investments.

question: How is the history of my non-ASB investments determined?
answer: Your non-ASB investment was recorded from the last time you updated it. Any new updates you make will be recorded in the graph as history.

question: How do KiwiSaver contributions work if I have more than one employer?
answer:
If you have more than one employer when you join KiwiSaver, you can choose whether you contribute from one, or all of those jobs. If you start new employment after joining KiwiSaver, you'll have to make contributions from your salary or wages from that job (unless you're entitled to a savings suspension). If you don't wish to make contributions from that new job, you'll need to apply to Inland Revenue for a savings suspension and let your new employer know that you are on a savings suspension. A savings suspension can be applied to any or all of your jobs.  Find out more about
savings suspension
here.

question: Why can’t I see my property value and range in Wealth Central or if they are incorrect?
answer:
Property details are missing when we are unable to match the property to our data sources.
For example: Legal description of 16 Smith Street doesn’t match to council details which have the address as 1/16 Smith Street and 2/16 Smith Street. This will be resolved as soon as our data sources are updated.
Find out more about
property information

question: Where do the property details for Wealth Central come from?
answer:
Property information has been sourced from CoreLogic NZ Ltd. The estimated value is not an accurate representation of the market value of the property and should not be relied upon as a professional valuation.
If you need to change your property details, please contact CoreLogic on 0800 355 355 or
update your home details
on CoreLogic's website.

question: What is Wealth Central?
answer:
Wealth Central is your home for tracking your savings and investments. Here you will have more clarity on your current financial position and the opportunities available to you. Through Wealth Central you can:
View all your investments in one place.
Explore other investment opportunities.
Track your existing investments.
Access more investment information.

question: How do I remove my trust loan in Wealth Central?
answer: Similar to Home Central, Wealth Central displays all loans associated with the borrowing parties by default. You have the option to hide your Trust loan in Wealth Central by selecting the ‘Hide / Include investments’ link in the ‘Review your investments’ section.

Note:
The value of these investments won’t be automatically updated and will require you to keep the value of these investments accurate and up to date.

question: How do I sell shares using Online Share Trading?
answer:
Share trading for the New Zealand Stock Exchange (NZX) mainboard and the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX). Once you are logged into your share trading account:
Select Place an order in the left hand menu, then follow the steps on the page:
Select Sell from the dropdown, then select the stock exchange on which you would like to trade.
Type the company name or stock code, select it from the dropdown and then select Next.
Enter the number quantity of shares you want to sell.
Choose your price – you can set a
limit price
or, during market hours, you can select
market price
.
For shares on the NZX only, you need to enter your FIN also known as your authorisation code.
Select the settlement account into which the proceeds should be deposited.
Enter your contact phone in case we need to contact you about your order.
Select Preview order.
Review the details of your order and if you are happy to submit it, select Proceed. If you do not wish to proceed you can select Cancel or you can change your order by selecting Amend.
Provided all order details are valid, your order will be sent to the market.
For more information see our guide on
how to buy and sell shares in Online Share Trading
.

question: How do I set up and use watchlists in Online Share Trading?
answer:
Log In to Online Share Trading, then to create new watchlist, select Watchlist in the left hand menu.
Beside the name of your default watchlist, select the + symbol and then enter a name for your new watchlist.
You can choose whether you would like your new watchlist to be your default by checking the box.
Once you select Done your new watchlist will appear.
To add a company to your watchlist, select Add new stock then type the name of the company or stock and select it from the dropdown. When you select Add stock, it will appear in your watchlist.
Your watchlist is a great way to keep track of the shares which interest you.  An icon will appear when a new recommendation or announcement is made for a particular stock on your watchlist.
You can have a maximum of 10 personalised watchlists active when you are in Online Share Trading.
You can add a maximum of 50 stocks per market (NZX and ASX) which will give you a maximum of 100 stocks per watchlist.
All pricing data is delayed by 20 minutes, so check the time stamp in the right hand corner to see what time the prices were last refreshed.
