question: What types of orders can I place when I'm share trading?
answer:
There are a number of different types of orders and instructions.  The main ones are market orders and limit orders.  Market orders are instructions to buy or sell shares at the prevailing trading price.  Limit orders are instructions to place your order when the price is within a limit you specify.  We also provide advanced orders (stop loss and target orders) for more experienced traders in the New Zealand and Australian market.
Let’s take a closer look:
Market orders:
These are orders to trade at the current market price and can be placed during market hours only. Market price is also known as the buyer's best available price. It’s the price the seller at the top of the order queue is asking, and vice versa if you are selling. Be aware the price is not guaranteed and may change in the time it takes to receive and validate your order instructions.
Limit orders:
A limit order is an instruction to buy or sell a stock at a specific price or better.  A buy limit sets the maximum price you are willing to pay and a sell limit sets the minimum price at which you are willing to sell. Setting a limit on your order ensures you do not trade outside a specific price range but it does not ensure that your order will be executed. Your order may be queued depending on the volume of shares being traded at the nominated price.
Stop Loss orders:
Setting a stop loss price allows you to indicate a price at which a sell order is activated and placed on the market. The stop loss order will only be 'triggered' if the share in question trades at the indicated price.  You can also specify a limit price, which means once the 'trigger' is activated, the shares will be sold at no less than this limit price.  Stop loss orders are aimed at traders who believe that if the price of a particular stock drops to a certain level, the share price will continue to fall. For that reason, the trigger price is always below the current market price.
Target Buy orders:
This indicates that a buy order should be activated if a share trades only at the selected price.  Setting a target price allows you to indicate a level at which a buy order should be activated and placed on the market. The target order will only be 'triggered' if the share in question trades at the indicated price. A limit price can also be indicated which means once the 'trigger' has been activated, the shares will be purchased at no more than this limit price.  The target order facility is aimed at traders who believe that if the price of a stock reaches a certain level, the share price will continue to rise. The trigger price is always above the current market price.
Please note
that stop loss, target or limit orders may remain on the market and queued for up to 20 business days including public holidays. For partially completed orders, we charge minimum brokerage on the first part and then if applicable, the outstanding brokerage is charged as the remainder of the order trades.
Find out more information on the
different types of share trading orders
in our guide.

question: I'm just getting started with share trading, how do I place an order?
answer:
This depends on the type of service you want to use at ASB Securities:
If you are share trading online and wish to place an online order, please log on to the ASB Securities website, click the Place an Order page which will take you through the steps to place an order. Otherwise you can call our share trading team on
0800 272 732
and someone will step you through the process.
If you are a telephone client, please call us on
0800 272 732
between 7am and 6pm (NZ time), Monday to Friday and we’ll be happy to help you.
What do I need to place my order to buy or sell a share?
To place an order to buy New Zealand or Australian shares online, you will need to log in to through the ASB Securities website, have sufficient funds in your Cash Management Account (CMA) to cover the purchase and then enter your order through the Place an Order page.
To place a sell order online or over the phone for the New Zealand market, you’ll need your valid Faster Identification Number (FIN).To place a sell order for the Australian market, online or over the phone, for shares purchased through ASB Securities or transferred into your ASB Securities account, simply log in or give us a call – there are no other details required.
Find out more information in our guide to
placing your first online order
.
To place all other orders, please phone us on
0800 272 732
.
Refresh page for YouTube video to render.

question: If I'm in Online Share Trading, am I able to generate reports for tax purposes?
answer:
No sorry. It's easy to export your portfolio information into Excel for personal use but there are no specific tax reporting tools available.

how to use your Portfolio in Online Share Trading
in our guide.

question: I'm share trading online and I'm trying to sell my NZ shares but I don't have a FIN number, what should I do?
answer:
NZ share registries issue your FIN (Faster Identification Number) which is like a Password for your shareholdings. The two main registries in NZ are Computershare (
0064 9 488 8700
) and Link Market Services (
0064 9 375 5999
).
Please call and they will advise how they can provide a copy by post.  If you need a FIN for urgent trading we can arrange an indemnity. This involves a service charge of $25.00 (including GST) for Computershare or $34.50 (including GST) for Link Market Services. To arrange an indemnity call ASB Securities on
0800 272 732
.
For more information see our guide on
how to buy and sell shares in Online Share Trading
.

question: Why am I receiving a custodial statement?
answer:
Regulations require statements to be sent to clients periodically, and require certain information to be detailed in the statements.
If you are a Nominee or Margin Lending client, you currently receive statements; we have made changes to the content and frequency.
If you are a custodial client (you have offshore holdings, excluding Australia), we have created a new report to keep you up to date with your holdings and transactions.

question: Will I receive my statement via email or print?
answer: You will receive your custodial statement by the same method you have elected to receive contract notes.

question: Why does ASB Securities not offer Dividend Reinvestment Plans (DRP) on Foreign Securities?
answer:
Effective from 1 May 2017 ASB Securities has discontinued offering the option to participate in Foreign Dividend Reinvestment Plans (DRP) for any stock held in custody, excluding Australia.
The decision has been made due to the complexity of foreign and domestic tax obligations when dealing with cash fractions as well as the decreasing number of DRP participants.
Find out how ASB Securities will handle your
Foreign Dividends
.

question: What is a bond's face value?
answer:
The face value of a bond is the initial investment amount (called the ‘principal’) that will be repaid to the investor at maturity.
Find out more about our
fixed interest securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: A company in which I own shares has had a share split. How can I adjust my holding in my portfolio within the Online Share Trading service?
answer:
Firstly, it's a good idea to check the company's announcements for the terms of the share split. When the share split is effective, it's a good idea to check the holding against the registry.
You'll then need to add a manual sell transaction in your portfolio to reduce the shares now cancelled, eg if you had 10,000 shares in a 1 for 10 share consolidation, you would create a manual sell transaction for 9,000 shares, giving you the correct holding of 1,000.
Find out
how to use your Portfolio in Online Share Trading
in our guide.

question: How does ASB Securities handle foreign dividends?
answer:
ASB Securities will receive all foreign dividends for stock held in custody (excluding Australia) in cash in the default payment currency, generally the home currency of where the stock is incorporated. We will deposit these funds into your attached Foreign Currency Account (FCA) or NZD Cash Management Account where an FCA is unavailable.
If you don't currently have an FCA find out more information and join online on our
Foreign Currency Account
page.

question: What updates will I receive about my investments in ASB Investment Funds?
answer:
Each year you will receive:
A statement every six months showing the value of your investment (and any transactions that have been made in the period covered by the statement).
A tax certificate.
A copy of ASB Investment Funds' annual report.
See the latest ASB Investment Fund updates
.
You can also see the
offer register and the scheme register
for more information about ASB Investment Funds (for example, ASB Investment Funds' financial statements).
You can contact the Registrar of Financial Service Providers for a copy of this information.
All information can be obtained free of charge.

question: What's the difference between primary and secondary share markets?
answer:
A primary market is where new securities are created and offered to the public (for example through an
Initial Public Offering or IPO
).
The secondary market is where previously issued securities (such as shares) are traded, i.e.: the stock exchange.
Find out more information on the
markets you can trade through ASB Securities
with our guide.

question: What are the Online Share Trading settlement terms?
answer:
Trades made on the New Zealand Stock Exchange (NZX) and Austrailian Stock Exchange (ASX) markets will settle two business days after the trade date (T+2). The settlement date is when sale proceeds will be paid to the seller and when the securities are registered into name of the buyer.
To ensure your trades settle on time, ensure that funds are available for any buy trade before settlement, and that all address details, FIN and registration details are correct for any sell trades.
For more information on the settlement period, view our guide to
placing your first online order
.

question: What is the primary market for bonds?
answer:
The primary market is where the Government or a company originally issues the bonds. In many cases, an initial issue is offered through a prospectus and investment statement (called ‘offer documents’). Terms of the sale are set out in the offer documents along with a complete description of the issuer and a range of associated statutory information that is required by law.
The secondary market is where you are purchasing bonds previously issued. This makes up the main market for bonds.
For more information visit our guide on
Fixed Interest Securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What is a capital note?
answer:
A capital note is a short-term bond issued by a company to help pay company liabilities.
When you buy a capital note, you’re loaning money to a company for a fixed period of time in return for regular interest payments on a fixed interest rate (called a ‘coupon rate’).
Capital notes are generally unsecured and ranked ‘subordinated debt’ – which means if the company folds, they have a lower priority of being repaid than bonds, but have a higher priority than ordinary shares.
Before maturity (called the ‘election date’), investors are usually offered new terms for reinvestment, or the capital notes may convert into ordinary shares, usually at a small discount to the market price. You could also choose to be paid back in full, plus the interest.
Find out more about our
fixed interest securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What is a Bond?
answer:
A bond is a debt instrument issued by a borrower and registered into the name of the investor, with a fixed interest rate (called a coupon rate) and a specific maturity date.
Bonds are issued when the Government, local authorities or companies wants to raise funds in the medium to long term. Most bonds are usually issued for periods longer than one year.
Find out more with our
introduction to fixed interest securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What are the main types of bonds?
answer:
New Zealand Government bonds
- The most actively traded (and the most liquid) bonds in New Zealand. The payment of the interest and the repayment of capital are guaranteed by the Government, making it the most secure form of investment in New Zealand.
Corporate bonds
- Companies issue bonds to raise funds at fixed interest rates. The benefits of investing in corporate bonds include higher yield (dependent on security) over Government bonds, a degree of liquidity and a variety of investment terms and maturities.
Capital notes
- Companies issue capital notes to raise funds at fixed interest rates to fund company liabilities. Capital notes are generally more risky than Government or corporate bonds, but the benefits include a much higher yield over Government bonds, some liquidity as most capital notes are listed on the New Zealand Stock Exchange, and a variety of investment terms and maturities.
Local authority bonds
– Investors may be able to buy bonds in some of the larger councils, such as Auckland, Christchurch and Dunedin, although in Christchurch and Dunedin the minimum investment is $100,000. Smaller councils may also issue bonds from time to time.
Find out more about
fixed interest securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What is the secondary market for bonds?
answer:
The secondary market allows bonds to be bought or sold by an investor before maturity at the current yield. The secondary market also helps promote bonds from the primary market.
There’s now a wide range of bonds available, with many of the non-Government bonds and capital notes listed on the New Zealand Debt Exchange. This has enabled investors to trade their bonds more easily before maturity.
Find out more with our
introduction to fixed interest securities
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What are the risks of investing in an Initial Public Offering (IPO) or new issue?
answer:
As well as the potential benefits, investing in an IPO or new issue does involve some level of risk. For example:
Volatility risk -
This is when the share price goes up and down. This could be due to factors such as changes in the company’s profits or changes in economic conditions. If share prices fell, you would see a change in the market value of your holdings but you wouldn’t actually lose money unless you sold your shares.
Absolute risk -
This is the risk that your shares will be worth nothing, for example if the company goes out of business.
It’s important to weigh up the risks before investing in an IPO or new issue. If you have any hesitation at all, then consider carefully if this type of investment is for you.
Find out
how to get started investing in new issues and IPOs
with our guide.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: How is a bond’s market value calculated?
answer:
Calculating the bond’s market value is a complicated process that involves comparing the coupon rate of a bond with the current interest rate and determining the cost per $100 of
face value
.
Find out more information on
new issues and IPOs
.
Interested in online share trading? Find out more at
ASB Securities
.

question: What am I able to trade through ASB Securities?
answer:
You can trade New Zealand Exchange (NZX), New Zealand Alternative Market (NZAX) and Australian Stock Exchange (ASX) listed equities online.
You are also able to trade in the
New Zealand Fixed Interest
, the New Zealand NXT Market and foreign markets (US, UK, Canadian and other overseas markets) only by telephone.
Find out more information on the
markets you can trade through ASB Securities
with our guide.

question: Do I need to be an ASB Securities customer to buy shares in a share offer?
answer:
For any broker offer we require you to be a customer, but for a public offer we can assist you but we recommend you do become one. It’s free to
join ASB Securities
and it may make things easier for you as we can keep you informed about the latest share offers and market news.
Who is eligible to buy shares in an Initial Public Offering (IPO)?
Typically various entities may be able to buy shares, such as individuals, companies, limited liability partnerships and managed funds (for example KiwiSaver funds). If there are specific eligibility criteria, these are made available when the share offer goes live. In some instances New Zealand offers can only be available to New Zealand residents.
What is the issue price of a share, what does that mean?
The issue price is the cost per share or unit when issued. This is set before the shares are listed on the share market
For more information
visit our guide on new issues and IPOs
.

question: I have bonds in my portfolio; will these trades feed in to my Online Share Trading Automated Portfolio?
answer:
Unfortunately no. Bond trades don't automatically feed in to your Automated Portfolio when traded over the phone with ASB Securities. However, you can manually add a bond trade using the Add function within the portfolio.
Unfortunately yield traded bonds will show incorrect movements in your portfolio and is not recommended to track these. The portfolio will display the current yield as a price, i.e. 5.04% will display as $0.0504
For more information
view out guide on how to understand the portfolios
.

question: How do I record a name change in Online Share Trading of one of the companies in which I hold shares?
answer:
If the ticker (trading) code remains the same, this change will happen automatically.
If the company has a new ticker code, how do I update my Online Share Trading portfolio?
If there is also a change to the ticker code, you'll need to add a manual entry.
In your portfolio, simply add a sell transaction to remove shares in the old code and add a buy transaction to reflect the shares under the new code. It's a good idea to check your holdings against the registries record for accuracy.

question: Can all my Online Share Trading portfolios have automatic trade feeds?
answer:
Yes. Orders traded via ASB's share trading service of NZX or ASX stocks will always feed automatically into the Automated Portfolio. You can choose for other portfolios to receive autofeeds under the Portfolio Summary page by selecting the Auto checkbox.
Check out this video for the simple steps to creating a new portfolio and getting the most out of the portfolio service:
Refresh page for YouTube video to render.

question: How do I settle in U.S. Dollars (USD) or any other currency for a NZX trade using Online Share Trading?
answer: Once you have progressed to the settlement account step of Place an Order, you will be able to select your USD Foreign Currency Account (FCA) or any other FCA you may have.

question: How can I switch ASB Investment Funds?
answer:
You can switch between funds by contacting the ASB Wealth Advisory Service on
0800 108 084
.
Please note, if you switch between funds, you must switch at least $500.
We can delay or suspend a switch in exceptional circumstances in accordance with the Trust Deed.

question: Who do I ask about tax implications of share trading?
answer:
Any income from shares or any other investments, including dividends or capital gains, will be liable for income tax. But how much tax you pay is entirely dependent on your personal circumstances. As such, ASB can't offer any tax advice in relation to your investment. We recommend you talk with a tax advisor or read more at
IRD.govt.nz

question: Why is it that I can only settle in New Zealand Dollars (NZD), Australian Dollars (AUD) and United States Dollars (USD) for my Australian orders when I’m using the Online Share Trading service?
answer:
Settlement of Australian orders in any other currency is unavailable online at the moment.
If you wish to transfer funds to another currency for your Australian order please contact one of our Online Brokers during business hours (7am to 6pm Monday to Friday)  on
0800 272 732
or
0064 9 448 8120
if you are calling from overseas.
Find out more information on the
markets you can trade through ASB Securities
with our guide.

question: I've come across some unfamiliar terms in share trading, what's a holder number?
answer:
If you don't have a Common Shareholder Number (CSN) but already hold shares, you will have been issued with a 'customer' or holder number. It identifies you and your current shareholding with a share registry. Your holding can then be transferred on to a CSN (that identifies all your share holdings across more than one share registry) by contacting your sharebroker.
Find out more information about a holder number in our guide to
placing your first online order
.

question: How do I buy Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs)?
answer:
Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) trade much like shares on the market. ETFs can be traded through Online Share trading on both the New Zealand Stock Exchange (NZX) and Australian Stock Exchange (ASX), or over the telephone.
Find out more about
Exchange Traded Funds
.

question: What is the minimum parcel of shares I can buy if I'm share trading through ASB Securities?
answer:
If you wish to buy shares and you are not already a shareholder in that company, you’ll need to buy a minimum number of shares. If you own the minimum holding already, then you can purchase any amount. The New Zealand Exchange (NZX) and the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX) have different rules with regard to minimum holdings.
Find out more information about the
minimum holding requirements
.

question: If I hold Australian shares under a Security Reference Number (SRN), how do I place a sell order over the internet?
answer:
If your Australian Stock Exchange (ASX) shares are held under a Security Reference Number (SRN) and you wish to sell, please call us on
0800 272 732
or
0064 9 448 8120
(from overseas).
You'll need to provide your client number and the SRN (this can be found on the most recent registry holding statement). We'll check the shareholding details to ensure they match your CHESS account by ownership and address. If both match we will connect the SRN to your account, which will allow you to place the order online within the next 20 business days. After this time the SRN will be removed from the trading system and you will need to contact us again.
Find out more with our guide to
placing your first online order
.

question: What is an index and how is it used?
answer:
An index is the combined value of a group of securities that’s used as a benchmark by investors to measure the performance of their own investment portfolios. An index works on the assumption that a sample of securities will replicate the whole market for those securities.
Examples of indexes include the NZX-50 – which replicates the performance of the New Zealand Stock Exchange’s 50 largest stocks, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) – which replicates the performance of 30 major stocks traded on the New York Stock Exchange and the Nasdaq.
Index tracking, also known as ‘passive management’, is an investment strategy that tracks the performance of an index. It involves investing in most or all of the securities within an index with the aim of maximising diversification, reducing risk and volatility, and improving returns over the long term.
Index tracking is generally less risky than the ‘active’ way of investing (where investors actively buy and sell stocks) and, because of its passive nature, the management fees for a passively-managed fund can also be lower.
Find out more about
Exchange Traded Funds
.

question: I'm starting share trading. What is a Faster Identification Number (FIN) and how does it work?
answer:
FIN stands for Faster Identification Number.  This is similar to a PIN on your bankcard and identifies you as the unique holder of those shares.  Your broker will need your FIN each time you make a sell transaction. (He or she may not keep your FIN beyond settlement of the transaction).  A FIN will be automatically assigned and sent to you separately by the share registry for safekeeping when you first buy shares.  If you want to sell your first share purchase prior to being registered and receiving a FIN, you will need to discuss the options with your broker before placing your sell order.
Find out more about
trading shares online with ASB Securities
.

question: I'm new to share trading, what is a Common Shareholder Number (CSN) and where can I find it?
answer:
A Common Shareholder Number (CSN) is a single shareholder number that is common to all New Zealand share registries for all listed securities. This number is how the registry identifies your holding as separate from any other. You’ll find your CSN on the right hand corner of any Statement of Holdings issued by a NZ share registry. If you don't already have a CSN leave the space in the application blank and ASB Securities will apply for one for you.
Find out more about
trading shares online with ASB Securities
.

question: What are the share market trading and ASB Securities business hours?
answer:
ASB Securities office hours are 7am to 6pm NZ time Monday to Friday.
NZX trading hours are between 10am to 5pm (only limit orders can be placed between 4.45pm and 5pm).  ASX trading hours are between 12pm and 6pm NZ time Monday to Friday.
These times can vary during daylight savings changeover. Other share market hours such as the US and UK markets are available on request.  Orders placed outside share market hours will be processed when the markets reopen.
Can I place an order when the market is closed?
Yes, you can place an online order outside market hours, or by calling ASB Securities during business hours.  Your order will be processed when trading opens on the next trading day.
For more information on the
markets you can trade with ASB Securities
.

question: How do I manually add or remove shares from my Online Share Trading portfolio?
answer:
You can manually add shares to or remove shares from your portfolio through adding a manual entry. Some of the reasons you would need to manually adjust your portfolio is where you have obtained shares outside of your share trading account, for example through an initial public offer (IPO), via a dividend reinvestment plan (DRP),  a share purchase plan (SPP), or through an off-market transfer.
You may also wish to update your portfolio holdings where there has been a corporate action such as a share consolidation, a share split or a stock (ticker) code change.
From the Transaction View or the Consolidated View screens, you simply click the Add button. To add shares to your Portfolio you will do a ‘Buy’ entry and to remove shares from your Portfolio you will need to do a ‘Sell’ entry
A pop-up box will then appear with various options for entering trade details, including contract and order number, trade date, type, quantity, stock, price, brokerage, other fees, FX rate, settlement date and notes. You only need to complete the fields with an asterisk
If you want to add multiple trades, the Confirm window will give you the option to Add another, or select OK to add a single entry
Trades will be clearly labelled as either Automated or Manual in the Transaction View or the View Entry screens of your portfolio
New to share trading? Read our guide on
how to start trading shares
.
Find out
how to use your Portfolio in Online Share Trading
in our guide.

question: I'm new to share trading, what is a share registry?
answer:
A share registry is an organisation which works on behalf of the listed company and amongst other things records any changes in share ownership, issues shareholding statements and manages dividend payments.
Find out more with our
how to start trading shares
guide.

question: How do I register my interest in buying shares in an Initial Public Offering (IPO) through ASB Securities?
answer:
Once an Initial Public Offering (IPO) is announced, there is usually a period where you will be able to register your interest.
Registering your interest doesn't mean you are making any commitment to invest, but it does put you on the list to receive the Offer Documents (typically a Prospectus and Investment Statement) when they are available to the public. These are the legal documents that may help you decide whether or not you want to invest.  It's also worth noting at this stage ASB Securities isn't under an obligation to fill your application, as we won't yet know the level of interest in the Initial Public Offering (IPO), or the availability of shares.
Why do I need to state a level of interest in an Initial Public Offering (IPO) ?
When you register your interest it is useful for us to know how much you want to invest to get a better idea about demand for the shares. You are not obliged to purchase to the level of interest you have indicated, or in fact to buy any shares.
So if I change my mind at this stage and decide I don’t want shares, that’s ok?
Yes. There is no obligation to buy shares when you register your interest. But once you've completed the application form to participate in the offer and it's been lodged with the share registry, you're not able to withdraw.
Find out more information on
new issues and IPOs
.

question: Where can I find my client number when I'm logged in to Online Share Trading?
answer:
Your six digit client number is located in the centre top part of each page underneath your account name and date.
Find out more information about
Online Share Trading
.

question: How can I make additional contributions to my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account to maximise the annual Government contribution?
answer:
It’s simple – you can top up any employee contributions with regular or lump sum contributions. You can do this by using FastNet Classic internet banking, mobile banking, calling 0800 ASB RETIRE (
0800 272 738
) or visiting an
ASB branch
. Our
saving more with KiwiSaver
guide has more information.

question: Can I access my money before the maturity date on a term deposit or ASB Term Fund?
answer:
Term deposit
With a term deposit you can access your money earlier, but you’ll have to give us 31 days’ notice or apply for an early withdrawal under
ASB’s hardship application process
. A reduced rate of interest will be applied to the amount withdrawn early.
ASB Term Fund account
You won’t be able to make an early withdrawal from an ASB Term Fund, except in exceptional circumstances approved by ASB. There will be a break fee if you withdraw early from an ASB Term Fund.

question: What happens to the insurance my Australian superannuation fund provides with my superannuation savings when transferring to KiwiSaver?
answer: If you have insurance cover attached to your Australian superannuation funds, this could cease when transferring your savings to a KiwiSaver scheme account. It makes sense to get independent insurance advice before applying for a transfer.

question: How do I find my savings, or find out if I’ve got any savings, in Australia?
answer:
Complete this
searching for lost super form
or, if you know your tax file number (TFN), use the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) SuperSeeker tool. To get your TFN, call
+61 2 6216 1111
and ask for personal tax enquiries: and be ready to provide your last known address in Australia.

question: What return will I get on my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme savings?
Can I transfer my savings in an existing superannuation scheme into the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme?
answer:
If you have savings in an existing superannuation scheme in New Zealand you will need to check with your existing superannuation scheme provider as to whether you are able to transfer your savings to the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme.
If you have savings in an Australian complying superannuation scheme, regulated by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) but now live in New Zealand permanently, then you can
transfer your savings to the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme
.
The ASB KiwiSaver Scheme does not currently accept superannuation transfers from countries other than Australia. For example, it does not accept transfers from UK pension schemes, as it is not a Qualifying Recognised Overseas Pension Scheme (QROPS).

question: How do I make KiwiSaver contributions?
answer:
Your employer will deduct your contributions from your after-tax salary or wages and send them, along with any employer contributions, to Inland Revenue. Inland Revenue will process and send these contributions to your KiwiSaver scheme provider. It usually takes around three months from the date of deduction from your salary or wages to the date your KiwiSaver scheme provider receives your contributions.
You can also make voluntary
contributions to your KiwiSaver account
at any time. With the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme, you can do this in many ways,
including via our online banking service FastNet Classic or using the ASB Mobile Banking app.
If you would like to track your contributions via Inland Revenue before they get to your KiwiSaver account, you can register at
www.kiwisaver.govt.nz
for the
My KiwiSaver
service.

question: What investments will a Wealth Manager recommend?
answer:
An ASB Wealth Manager can advise on a range of financial products and services, however the ASB Portfolio Series discretionary investment management service and the ASB Investment Funds form the core investment product and service options.
Disclosure information
on these products and services is available.
ASB Wealth Managers' disclosure statements are available on request and free of charge from your ASB Wealth Manager.

question: Can I use my KiwiSaver savings to buy an investment property?
answer: No. You can only make a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal.

question: How do I invest in ASB Portfolio Series?
answer:
An ASB Wealth Manager will meet with you and ask you questions about your circumstances and advise you on your investment options. Your ASB Wealth Manager may recommend that you join ASB Portfolio Series.
See our
What a Wealth Manager can do for me
guide.

question: What happens when I meet with a Wealth Manager?
answer:
You’ll have several meetings with your Wealth Manager so they can get to know your personal goals and circumstances and explain how ASB’s Wealth Advisory Service works.
If you want to continue, they’ll develop an investment plan that includes recommendations for financial products and services. Please see our
What a Wealth Manager can do for me
guide for more detail on the wealth advice process.

question: I’ve seen Canstar ratings for some of the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme funds – who is Canstar?
answer: Canstar is a company that researches and rates different financial services from New Zealand banks and institutions. Canstar has created a five-star rating system to help people understand and compare different products and services.

question: Some of the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme funds invest in equities, what are these?
answer: Equities (also known as shares) represent a share of ownership in a company or other entity. Investors can earn returns from shares in the form of dividends and capital growth. Returns on shares may also be negative.

question: What is diversification and how does it impact my KiwiSaver investment?
answer: Diversification means allocating your investment into different asset classes such as equities, property, fixed interest and cash. Diversifying your investment across different asset classes helps to smooth out the short-term ups and downs in a fund due to market movements. The intention is that the positive performance of some investments can help offset negative or lesser performance of other assets.

question: Can I make withdrawals from ASB Portfolio Series?
answer: Yes, you can make regular or partial withdrawals from your ASB Portfolio Series portfolio. Some requirements must be met before you can make withdrawals (for example, minimum withdrawal amounts apply). If you wanted to make a withdrawal from your portfolio, you would talk with your ASB Wealth Manager.

question: Can I choose my investments in ASB Portfolio Series?
answer: You can make decisions about adding money to, or withdrawing money from, your portfolio. However, we will make decisions about your financial products and how they are managed.

question: How are my investments held in ASB Portfolio Series?
answer: A custodian will hold your financial products on your behalf. The custodian for ASB Portfolio Series is currently Investment Custodial Services Limited.

question: What is ASB Portfolio Series?
answer: ASB Portfolio Series is a discretionary investment management service (DIMS) provided by ASB Bank Limited.  Under a DIMS, the provider invests your money on your behalf in financial products, such as shares, bonds and managed investment products, and charges fees for the service.

question: When investing my money, how long do I need to invest for?
answer:
You’ll need to have a long-term investment timeframe.
The funds you have available to invest should form part of your long term savings, with no immediate requirements for the money.

question: How much will my Australian superannuation funds be worth when converted into New Zealand dollars?
answer:
Your final transfer amount will depend on the exchange rate. Usually your Australian provider will pay by International Money Transfer, or by cheque in Australian dollars. Typically, once we receive it, we provide same-day cleared funds. Then, the following business day, we use the applicable exchange rate to convert the amount to New Zealand dollars.
If the money is sent in Australian dollars, it’s usual for the receiving bank (ASB) to build a currency conversion fee (to $NZD) into the exchange rate.  If the money is sent in New Zealand dollars, your Australian provider and/or their bank may have charged for doing this.
Just note that exchange rate changes can happen during the transfer process. If the exchange rate does change, it will affect the value of your retirement savings.

question: Can I use the money transferred from my Australian superannuation to buy my first home?
answer: Because of Australian superannuation fund rules, savings from an Australian complying superannuation fund aren’t available for a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal or the home deposit subsidy. However, any earnings on the transferred amount can be withdrawn once you’re eligible to make a KiwiSaver first home withdrawal.

question: How do I find out how much I have contributed to my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme since 1 July?
answer:
There are two easy ways:
Use
FastNet Classic
ASB’s internet banking service to see any voluntary (lump sum) and/or regular contributions you’ve made during the year.
Call Inland Revenue on 0800 549 472 (0800 KIWISAVER) or log in to the Inland Revenue My KiwiSaver service at
kiwisaver.govt.nz
to check your salary and wage contributions.

question: What happens if I don't select an employer- chosen KiwiSaver scheme for my business?
answer: If you haven’t elected an employer chosen scheme and your employee hasn’t selected a KiwiSaver scheme to join, Inland Revenue will allocate them to one of the default KiwiSaver schemes.

question: What happens at the end of the tax year if my Prescribed Investor Rate is 0%?
answer: Investors with a 0% Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR) will need to file a tax return with Inland Revenue directly in respect of your Portfolio Investment Entity (PIE) investment and settle any tax liability. Your annual tax statement we send you will have the information you need.

question: How do I consolidate my shareholding into one Common Shareholder Number (CSN)?
answer:
Call us on 0800 272 732 weekdays 7am-6pm, or download the off-market transfer from
Computershare
or
Link Market Services
. Return your completed forms to them for processing.

question: What are the risks from fixed interest investment?
answer:
As with any investment, there are a number of risks you should consider. Some of the risks include:
Credit risk:
The potential for a partial or total loss of capital and interest due to a default by the borrower.
Liquidity risk:
The potential for loss though the inability to easily trade a security in order to convert that security to cash, should the funds be required before the debt matures.
Interest rate risk:
The potential for loss on the capital value of a security due to an upward movement in interest rates. Rising interest rates cause the price or present value of a debt security to fall. This will generate a capital loss for the investor, if the security is sold before maturity.
Reinvestment risk:
Fixed interest securities are valued on the assumption that all future cash flows are reinvested at the original investment interest rate or yield. Falling interest rates would see future cash flows reinvested at a lower rate, which would generate a lower return from the security.
While these are some of the risks of investing in fixed interest, this answer does not constitute advice. You must assess your own risk before choosing to invest.

question: I'm in Online Sharetrading, what do I need to know about market orders?
answer:
Market orders can only be placed during market hours.
Market orders can be placed but may not be accepted when trading in a particular stock is halted/suspended.
Market orders cannot be amended or cancelled during market hours.
A market order may be placed on a limit price if we believe the spread between the bid and ask price is too great.  If this is the case we will try to contact you by phone or email. However, it is at our discretion how we place the market order on market.

question: I'm looking at investing in bonds, what is the coupon rate?
answer:
The coupon rate is the original interest rate the issuer agreed to pay to investors and is fixed at the date the bond is first issued. This is payable half yearly or quarterly in arrears by the share registry (in New Zealand, the two main Registries are Link Market Services and Computershare) and can be mailed directly to the registered owner or direct credited to the registered owner's bank account.
This is not necessarily the return you receive on your investment. The return you receive ('yield to maturity') is determined by the premium, or discount to face value that you pay for a specified parcel of bonds.

question: Can I change KiwiSaver schemes at any time?
answer: Yes. You can apply to have your savings transferred to another KiwiSaver scheme whenever you like. Some providers may charge you a fee to transfer; the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme does not charge a fee for KiwiSaver transfers.

question: Why do I have more than one Common Shareholder Number (CSN)?
answer:
If you have an existing Common Shareholder Number (CSN) and apply for a sharetrading account without recording your number on your new account application, you may be issued with another CSN. You can also hold multiple CSNs under different names, for example you have one CSN for individual shareholdings and another CSN for joint shareholdings that you own with another person.
Find out how to
consolidate your shareholding under one CSN number
.

question: I received Dividend Reinvestment Plan (DRP) shares. How do I add or update these in my Online Sharetrading portfolio?
answer:
You can add these shares manually in either the Transaction View or the Consolidated View screens by clicking the Add button.
A pop-up box will then appear with various options for entering trade details, including contract and order number, trade date, type, quantity, stock, price, brokerage, other fees, FX rate, settlement date and notes.

question: What is the market price of a share?
answer: The market price is the current going price the share can be bought or sold on the share market. This may well be different from the issue price which is set before the float, or initial launch of the share.

question: Do I need to file a tax return for my Portfolio Investment Entity (PIE) income?
answer:
If your Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR) was recorded correctly for the year, the correct amount of Portfolio Investment Entity (PIE) tax will be paid to Inland Revenue on your behalf and you don’t need to do anything further. You can check the PIR we have recorded for you using the ASB Mobile Banking app or FastNet Classic internet banking or by
contacting us
.
ASB Mobile app:
Log in to the ASB Mobile app.
Tap the profile icon in the top left corner of the ‘Accounts’ screen.
Tap ‘Personal profile’.
Then scroll down and open ‘Tax details’ to view or update.
FastNet Classic:
Log in to your FastNet Classic account.
Click on 'Settings' in the top right of the page.
Click on ‘Personal profile’.
Scroll down and click 'Tax details' to see your tax details.
Find out more information about PIRs
and if your PIR is recorded incorrectly, please let us know so we calculate the correct amount of tax for you next year.
If the PIR information we held for you was incorrect in the current tax year, you may need to complete a tax return.
If the PIR we held for you should have been a lower PIR and tax has been deducted at a higher PIR, then from the tax year ending 31 March 2021 the overpaid tax will be refunded by the IRD either as the result of the Inland Revenue’s year-end automated income assessment process, or through the completion of an income tax return.
If the PIR we held for you should have been higher, the additional PIE tax will be calculated either as the result of the Inland Revenue’s year-end automated income assessment process, or through the completion of an income tax return.

question: What’s the best ASB KiwiSaver Scheme fund for me?
answer:
The best fund for you is an individual decision; it should be based on your savings goals (like buying your first home or retirement) and when you plan to use your money (your investment timeframe), and your risk tolerance and apetite.
Every investment has some level of risk, which means ups and downs in your balance are expected. Our lower risk funds have smaller movements up and down in value and are suitable for someone that is looking to access their savings sooner.
Our higher risk funds are likely to experience larger movements up and down in value but aim to deliver higher potential returns over the long term. So, if it's twelve years or more before you'll need to access your KiwiSaver savings, you might consider taking more risk. As you get closer to accessing your KiwiSaver savings (for retirement or buying your first home), you should consider stepping down your risk with a less aggressive fund.
Use our simple tool that makes choosing a fund easy.
This information does not have regard to the financial situation or needs of any reader. As individual circumstances differ, you should seek appropriate professional advice.

question: What ASB KiwiSaver Scheme fund am I automatically enrolled in and why?
answer:
If you were automatically enrolled in the ASB KiwiSaver Scheme (by your employer), we invest you in our Conservative Fund.
We use the Conservative Fund as it has a high proportion of lower risk assets and therefore smaller ups and downs in your balance are expected. But low risk means it is likely to deliver lower potential returns over the long term.
You can choose any one of our seven funds.
Use our simple tool that makes choosing a fund easy
. You can switch funds anytime in FastNet Classic internet banking.

question: What is an ASB KiwiSaver Scheme fund?
answer:
We have seven funds you can invest in. Each one has a different investment objective and strategy.
Some are conservative, and aim to reduce ups and downs in your balance. That means they have lower potential returns over time.
Others focus on higher potential growth. You may experience more ups and downs in your balance but with a longer investment timeframe there is time to ride them out.
There are other funds that aim to provide moderate potential returns, with some ups and downs in your balance.
It's important to choose the fund that's right for you. You can switch funds now in FastNet Classic internet banking. If you need help choosing a fund use our simple
Help me choose tool
.
If you haven't chosen a fund you are automatically invested in our Conservative Fund, which may not be right for you and could mean you miss out on thousands of dollars in retirement.

answer:
If you’re not sure which fund you want to change to,

If you have access to FastNet Classic internet banking, you'll see your ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account on the 'Balances' page. Click on the 'Options' button next to that to open a drop down menu. (You can also find the 'Change Fund' option on your Investment Overview page.) Select the third option down 'Change Fund'.

changing your fund form
and post it back to us.
It will take 3-5 days to appear in FastNet Classic or the ASB Mobile Banking app and we will send you a confirmation when this has been done.

question: Can I make a contribution into my ASB KiwiSaver Scheme account from another bank?
answer:

Upon authorising the changes made will take immediate effect.

When an alert is saved it will appear under your Current Alerts page

Log into FastNet Business

question: What happens once a User / Administrator profile has been deleted in FastNet Business?

Support Finder

question: What is Support Finder?

Support Finder is a digital tool within the ASB Mobile Banking app to help customers identify the benefits and services that the government provides within minutes. It was created by ASB to help customers find out what financial assistance they could be eligible for and how they can apply.

Support Finder

Support Finder

question: What is my Personal Profile?
answer:
Your Personal Profile is a place where you can let us know your most up to date personal and contact details. This helps us to contact you sooner if we have any urgent news and provide you with an efficient banking experience.
It is also a place where you can verify your home address and your identity that helps us comply with New Zealand's
Anti-Money Laundering (AML)
laws, and protect you and ASB.
Updating your Personal Profile is a simple process that you can do through FastNet Classic internet banking or the ASB Mobile Banking app at any time.

question: What are the benefits of completing my Personal Profile?
answer:
Completing your Personal Profile helps us to contact you sooner if we have any urgent news and address you in the way you prefer. It also ensures we comply with New Zealand’s
Anti-Money Laundering (AML)
laws by confirming your address, identity and occupation.

question: What are the different types of limits in FastNet Business?
answer:
FastNet Business Limits
The following limits apply to all FastNet Business sites
Account Limit (for all FastNet Customers)
International Limit (For FastNet International Customers)
Receipts Limits (Optional for Direct Debit / Receipts Customers)
User Limits (for all FastNet Customers)
Daily Account Limits
Is a daily pay away limit on all of your actual account(s) accessed in FastNet Business. This limit is loaded at an account stem and covers all suffixes within an account.
The daily account limit applies to all payments, including any International Money Transfers processed from a standard NZD account.
Funds Transfers between various suffixes on the same account number is not included in the daily limit.
International Division Limit
The International limit is based on a NZ Dollar value and covers all International Money Transfers processed from a Foreign Currency account.
(NB: A Foreign Currency Out-of-Hours Limit of NZD$100,000 applies outside of business hours, during weekends and during public holidays)
User Limits
This is a daily limit that individual administrators and  users can pay away per day using FastNet Business. The user limits applies to ALL payment types excluding Funds Transfers when transferring within the same unique account number.
A user’s limit can be overridden by the approved account, international or receipts limits. For example, if a user has a $100,000 daily limit and attempting to pay away $80,000 against an account with an approved limit of $50,000. The payment will not process due to the client exceeding their daily approved account limit.
Limits are verified at the time of:
- Saving a payment (you will receive a warning message)
- Authorising and sending a payment (you will receive an error message advising you to phone the FastNet Business Helpdesk – at this stage your payment will not process without intervention.

question: Why haven't I received the verification code for my Personal Profile?
answer:
If you haven’t received your verification code after entering your email address, please ensure that:
You have spelled your email correctly.
You have access to your email inbox.
The email is not in your spam folder.
You have waited at least 2 minutes.
If you haven’t received your verification code after entering your phone number, please ensure that:
You have entered your phone number correctly.
You have mobile phone coverage.
The number you used wasn’t an international phone number (only NZ mobile numbers can be verified).
You have waited at least 2 minutes.

question: Why do I need to verify my phone number for my Personal Profile?
answer: Verifying your phone number helps us to confirm your identity and provides us with the best way to send important and helpful messages sent straight to your phone.

question: Why do I need to verify my email address for my Personal Profile?
answer: Verifying your email address helps us to confirm your identity and provides us with the best way to send you helpful and important messages straight to your inbox.

You can choose to continue to adding all your payees to the one all payees group, or you can create a new library group to make it easier to group your payments.

question: Can I add duplicate payees to a library in FastNet Business?

question: How do I set or change a User limit in FastNet Business?
answer:
This change can only be made by one of your FastNet Business site administrators. (Note: An administrator cannot increase a daily user’s limit higher then what they have set for themselves).
Go into the administration section of FastNet Business:
- Users
- Click on User profiles
- Select the user in question by clicking on the blue hyperlink of their user ID
Upon clicking on the user ID you will be taken into the users’ profile, defaulting to the Access tab.
Under security:
- Change the user limit
- Save & exit
Once saved, your change is ready for authorisation and you will be taken back to the User Profiles main screen, listing all your users.
- Click on ‘Current User Request
- Select the user by clicking in the tick box to the left hand side of their name
- Click authorise
- Enter in your own user credentials and Netcode token number
- Click authorise
Upon authorising the changes you need to log out and log in to update the limits.

question: How do I review my remaining daily limits in FastNet Business?
answer:
There are a few ways to check daily account and user limits, depending on what information you are looking for.
To check what your daily account limits are:
Under the administration section of FastNet Business
- Click on Limits
- Click on Account Limits
This will display your daily limits against each account
To check what your daily user limits are:
Under the administration section of FastNet Business
- Click on Users
- Click on User Profiles
- Select the user in question by clicking on the blue hyperlink of their user ID
- You will default to the Access tab and the daily limit is found under the security section
If you are wanting to check limits against all users. You can continue to follow the instructions above or if you can click on the ‘User Profiles Report’ on the left hand side menu under the Administration / Users section.
This report will list all users on your FastNet Business site which will include their daily user limits, hours of access, permissions, account access etc.
To view what limits you have remaining
On the authorisation page of your payments is a hyperlink to ‘view daily limits’
- Click on the hyperlink
You can search for either daily account and / or user limits
- Select what limits you are wanting to check
- Click on Display Limits
The daily limits will display below

question: How do I change User levels of access in FastNet Business?
answer:
There are two types of users on FastNet Business:
Administrator
User
If you wish to change an administrator to a user or vice versa, please complete a FastNet Business User Change Request form and send to the FastNet Business Helpdesk. Contact details for the Helpdesk are found at the top of the form.

question: How do I remove a User’s Permission/Account Access in FastNet Business?
answer:
To change / remove permissions or account access for a user or administrator, go back into the users profile and click on the appropriate tabs (Accounts / Permissions).
Unselect the options you want removed
Save
Authorise
Changes will take immediate effect.
Note:
Any future dated payment authorised by a user who has lost their authorise permissions or account access to the payment deduction account will fail.

question: How do I delete a User profile in FastNet Business?
answer:
When a user profile has been deleted, it will be removed from the User Profiles – Users list. As a result, it:
Can never be used to log into FastNet Business.
Cannot be copied or reinstated.
Any future dated payment authorised by a deleted user will not process.
Note: An Administrator's user profile can only be deleted by the FastNet Business Help Desk.
On the FastNet Business tab bar, click Administration
On the left-menu, click User Profiles
On the User Profiles – Users page, select the relevant user profile using the radio buttons, then at the bottom of the page, click Delete User.
4. Click the Current User Requests tab.
5. Click the check box for each request that you wish to authorise, then click Authorise.
6. Complete the User ID, Password and Netcode fields, and then click Authorise.

question: Is there a limit to the amount I can deposit at an ASB instant deposit ATM?
answer:
You can deposit up to 50 notes (of any denomination) per transaction when inserting your ASB card at the ATM.
Larger amounts can be deposited over the counter at any
ASB branch.

question: How can I contact FastNet Business?
answer:
​You can call the FastNet Business Help Desk on
0800 22 55 27
(+64 9 623 8763) from 8:00am to 6:30pm Monday to Friday.

question: When should I use the FastNet Business Change form?
answer:
Use the
FastNet Business Change Request Form
(PDF) when you need to make any changes to your existing FastNet Business client, including:
Updating the details for your main FastNet Business contact.
Adding or Deleting an Administrator.
Transfering Administrator to User.
Adding or Deleting account(s).
Changing an Administrator’s User Limit.
Changing Existing Daily Account Limits.
Issuing new or replacement Netcode tokens.
Reallocating one or more of your current Netcode tokens to different users.
Adding International, Financial Markets, Card Manager and Professional Trust Modules.
Adding or Deleting IP Address for Verification.
Note:
To make changes to accounts you are not a signatory of on your FastNet Business Client, use the
Additional Account Form
(PDF) instead.
You can complete the PDF form on-screen and then print and sign it.
Send the signed form to FastNet Business using one of the following options:
Scan and email it to FNBHelpDesk@asb.co.nz.
Fax it to the FastNet Business Help Desk on 09 337 2102.
Post it to the FastNet Business Help Desk, P O Box 35, Shortland Street, Auckland 1140.
Not sure which form to use?
Call the FastNet Business Help Desk on 0800 22 55 27 (or +64 9 623 8763 from overseas), from 8:00am - 6:30pm, Monday to Friday.

question: Having trouble logging in to FastNet Business?
answer:
To log in to FastNet Business:
Type your FastNet Business client ID. Your client ID is a number assigned to your business.
Type your FastNet Business user ID. Your user ID is unique to you and does not change. User IDs have eight alphanumeric characters, and must contain at least two letters and two numbers.
Type your FastNet Business password. Your password must contain eight to ten characters, is case sensitive, and must contain at least two letters and two numbers.
Click Log In.
If you still cannot log in, refer to the information below for further assistance.
Forgotten Your User Password?
You have three attempts to enter your user password before your user profile is deactivated. If this occurs, you’ll need to reactivate it and, if necessary, reset your password. You can do this by contacting your FastNet Business Administrator.
Note:
A FastNet Business Administrator can reset a user's password and reactivate that user's profile in Administration > User Profiles. (If the user's profile has access to two-to-sign or three-to-sign accounts, other administrators may also be required to fully authorise the request.)
If you require further assistance, please phone the FastNet Business help desk on 0800 22 55 27 (+64 9 623 8763) from 8:00am - 6:30pm Monday to Friday.
Forgotten Your Administrator Password?
You have three attempts to enter your administrator password before your user profile is deactivated. If this occurs, you’ll need to reactivate it and, if necessary, reset your password.
Another Administrator can activate a one-to-sign account but two administrators are needed to reactivate a two-to-sign account.
To Reset an Administrator's Password:
If your account manadate is one-to-sign and you are the signatory of the account, please phone the FastNet Business help desk on 0800 22 55 27 (+64 9 623 8763) Monday to Friday, 8:00am - 6:30pm).
If your account mandate is two-to-sign or the FastNet Business help desk are unable to identify you, you'll need to follow the process below:
1. Complete the
Password Reset Form
(PDF). This must be signed by the relevant account signatories of the main account registered for FastNet Business.
2. Return the completed form using one of the following options:
- Scan and email it to FNBHelpDesk@asb.co.nz.
- Fax it to the FastNet Business help desk on 09 337 2102.
Note:
Completed forms received during help desk hours (8:00am - 6:30pm Monday to Friday) are normally processed within one hour.
Result:
The FastNet Business help desk will call with a temporary password and a FastNet Business confirmation message will be sent to your administrators.
3. Log into FasNet Business with the temporary password. You will be prompted to change you password. Click Acknowledge when you receive the confirmation message.
Changing Your Password - All Administrators and Users
FastNet Business will prompt all administrators and users to change their password. The frequency of your required password change depends on what your company has requested. The default change period is 90 days, but this can be updated to 180 days by an account signatory contacting the FastNet Business help desk.
If you are prompted to change your password, complete all the fields then click OK.
Your password:
must be eight to ten characters in length
must contain at least two alphabetic and two numeric characters
is case sensitive.
cannot be the same as the past 20 used passwords
Note:
You are not prompted to change your password every time you log in.

question: How do I set up Xero account feeds in FastNet Business?
answer:
Our account feeds can send your ASB transactions to Xero every day, automatically. If you use FastNet Business, here's how to set them up online. (If you use FastNet Classic,
follow these instructions
instead. We also provide transaction feeds for MYOB.)
Before you start
The first step is to set up your ASB accounts in Xero. Then you'll be able to choose them as the destination for your daily transactions.
It’s easy to do. For details, see
Xero's help article on setting up new accounts
.
Activating your feeds
Once you've logged in to FastNet Business:
1. Go to Administration.
2. Then 'Client.
3. Then 'Accounting Set Up'.
4. Choose 'Xero' from the pull down menu.
FastNet Business will show you a list of all your ASB accounts and cards that you can feed to Xero.
5. Tick the ones you want to update in Xero. There’s no cost for adding accounts, and you can turn off your feeds at any time.
6. You can back date your account feeds. The default is 1 April, which for most businesses is the start of the financial year.
7. You can also choose accounts to use with Direct Payment Link. This lets you choose the bills you want to pay in Xero, and upload the details directly to FastNet Business, ready for authorisation.
8. When you’ve made your selections, confirm you’re happy for us to send your information to Xero, and click OK.
You’ll then be taken to a Xero authentication screen. This confirms you have permission to add your account information to Xero.
Log in with your Xero details.
Allocate your new ASB transaction feeds to your ASB accounts in Xero.
Click OK, and you’re done.
Your first transaction feed will appear in Xero the next morning. Depending on how far back you backdated your feeds, your first report may include a lot of transactions.
Problems?
If you’ve run into any issues on the ASB side, just give our FastNet Business help desk a call on 0800 22 55 27. If you have any Xero issues, it’s best to contact them directly via their
support page
.

question: Can I install the ASB Mobile Business App on my phone and on my tablet?
answer: To ensure your security, we only allow one device per user. That means you can’t link to the same FastNet Business site on a phone on more than one device.  A future release will allow multiple site registrations on one device, i.e. if you are a user of site A and site B you will be able to login to both via a single app.

question: How far back can I view on my transaction history in FastNet Business?
answer:
Standard ASB accounts
7 years of transaction history are available through FastNet Business, limited to searching / viewing of 90 days at any one time.
Credit Cards
3 months of transactional history is available on FastNet Business.
Foreign Currency Accounts
Twenty-four months of transaction history on Foreign Currency accounts is available through FastNet Business, limited to searching / viewing of 100 days at any one time.
** To keep an ongoing transactional history we recommend you export your balances and transaction history on a regular basis.  You can also set up automated transaction feeds to your accounting software (such as Xero).

question: What kind of EFTPOS machines can I get for my business?
answer:
ASB has EFTPOS terminals to suit the way you do business. See
ASB Accept
for further information.

question: Can I get an EFTPOS machine for my healthcare practice from ASB?
answer:
When your clients want to pay by EFTPOS, ASB has an in-store terminal available called
ASB Accept Standalone
.
ASB Accept Standalone provides
ASB’s 7-Day settlement service
with electronic payments deposited to your account every day, including weekends and public holidays.

question: What are the FastNet Business alert operating hours?
answer:
Alerts are sent in real-time during the following operating hours (NZST):
Contact Device
Operating Hours (NZST)
Email
6.00 am to 11.00 pm, 7 days
Text message to a mobile phone
8.00 am to 10.00 pm, 7 days
Note: Foreign Exchange TXT and email alerts are only sent during trading hours (8.00 am to 5.50 pm, business days)
If alerts are triggered outside operating hours they are sent when operating hours commence the following morning.
Alerts are sent subject to the recipient (user) having the correct permissions and an active contact device. Audit logs provides further detail about sent alerts.
Alerts may sometimes be delayed, or not delivered, due to issues with your mobile or email provider.
Find out how to
create an alert in FastNet Business
.

question: How do I set up an MYOB bank feed from FastNet Business?
answer:
Our account feeds can send your ASB transactions to MYOB every day, automatically. If you use FastNet Business, here's how to set them up online. (If you use FastNet Classic,
follow these instructions
instead. We also provide
transaction feeds for Xero
.)
Before you start
The first step is to set up your ASB accounts in MYOB. Then you’ll be able to choose them as the destination for your daily transactions.
It's easy to do. For details, see
MYOB's help article on setting up new accounts
. In their process, MYOB gives you an eight digit number. Take a note of it, as you'll need it to complete your set up in FastNet Business.
Activating your feeds
Once you've logged in to FastNet Business:
1. Go to Administration
2. Then 'Client'
3. Then 'Accounting Set Up'
4. Choose 'MYOB' from the pull down menu
5. Enter your eight digit MYOB code.
FastNet Business will show all your ASB accounts and cards that support transaction feeds.
6. Tick the ones you want to update in MYOB. There's no cost for adding accounts, and you can turn off your feeds at any time.
7. You can back date your account feeds. The default is 1 April, which for most businesses is the start of the financial year.
8. When you've made your selections, confirm you're happy for us to send your information to MYOB, and click OK.
MYOB will then complete the set up at their end, and email you when your bank feeds are ready. Then you can
link the bank feeds in AccountRight
, and you're done.
Problems?
If you run into any issues on the ASB side, just give our FastNet Business help desk a call on 0800 22 55 27. If you have any MYOB issues, it's best to contact them directly via
their support page
.

question: Can I use FastNet Business with my payroll package?
answer:
Yes. FastNet Business supports a range of file formats for use with other applications. For more information on supported files, refer to the
File Formats Technical Guide.
You can also contact your support team or the help system for your payroll/accounting package to see if it can:
Accept transaction history export files to assist with bank reconciliation and/or
Generate a bulk payment/direct debit file that can be imported into ASB FastNet Business to save time when creating transactions.

question: How do I cancel my FastNet Business account?
answer:
To cancel a FastNet Business account please send a written request to
FNBhelpdesk@asb.co.nz
signed by an account signatory(s).

question: Are there any solutions my business can use for managing client funds held in a trust?
answer:
Professional Trust is an optional FastNet Business internet banking module that gives organisations charged with managing client funds the ability to open interest-bearing client accounts, make deposits and withdrawals, credit automatically-generated interest income, provide statements to clients and close accounts. Learn more about
Professional Trust
.

question: Can FastNet Business internet banking help me to manage approvals from several stakeholders?
answer:
FastNet Business internet banking
provides a fast and efficient way to manage the banking requirements of more complex organisations – including enabling more than one person to authorise payments.

question: How do I set up an accounting package from FastNet Business?
answer:
FastNet Business enables you to set up daily automated transaction feeds to your accounting software package (such as Xero and MYOB). Please note this function can only be completed by a FastNet Business administrator.
You can set up automatic transaction feeds from the following types of accounts:
Transaction and savings accounts
Business and personal loans
Term deposits
Credit cards
Foreign currency accounts
You can choose the date to start the transaction feed. This can be up to 12 months in the past, enabling you to include transactions from the start of your financial year.
Note:
At present, you can only set up transactions feeds to Xero & MYOB. We expect to add support for other third party accounting software packages in the near future.
Below are the instructions on how to set up your daily automated transactions feeds:
-
Xero
-
MYOB

question: What do I need to get started with the ASB Mobile Business App?
answer:
You need to be enabled to have the mobile app access against your user profile via the Administration section of FastNet Business.
You can check your User Preferences to see if you’re already enabled: go to Administration > User Profiles, and click on your User Name. The Mobile access setting is at the bottom of the first tab, ‘Access’.
If your access is disabled, one of your site Administrators will be able to enable and authorise access for you by:
Go to the administration section of FastNet Business
Click on Users
Click on User Profiles
Select the user by clicking on the blue user ID
You will default to the Access page. Scroll down to ASB Mobile Business Access
Click Enabled
Save and Exit
This change will now need to be authorised
Click the Current User Requests tab.
Select the check box for each request that you wish to authorise,
Click Authorise.
Complete the User ID, Password and Netcode fields
Click Authorise.

question: How do I apply to have Direct Debits in FastNet Business?
answer:
To have the ability to create a direct debit file to pull money from other accounts, you must be a DD Initiator.
Further instructions around
signing up to process Receipts (Direct Debits)
.
As part of this process you will also have the option to use FastNet Business to process your receipt payments.

question: What information will the Direct Debit Dishonour Report give me in FastNet Business?
answer:
If any of the payers in a receipt, for example, do not have sufficient funds, the money cannot be deducted from their accounts. If this is the case, your account will not be credited with these funds and a dishonour report will be sent to your FastNet Business Inbox.
The dishonour report identifies all payers whose accounts could not be debited, along with the reason.
Dishonour reports provide the following information about a receipt:
Receipt ID
Registration ID
Your account number
Note:
On the report, this is called the contra account.
Receipt due date
A list of the payer transactions that have been dishonoured.
The receipt's total amount (Input)
Of the receipt's total amount:
how much has been deposited into your account (Paid), and
how much has not been deposited into your account (Dishonoured).
The reason for dishonoured transactions.

question: I have a new replacement phone, how do I set up the ASB Mobile Business app?
answer: Download the app and register your new phone. Because you can only operate ASB Mobile Business on one device at a time, you will be asked to accept the deregistration of your old phone.

question: How do I install the ASB Mobile Business App?
answer:
Once your access is enabled, download ‘ASB Mobile Business’ from either the iTunes Store for your iPhone, or Google Play for your Android.
(Note: ASB Mobile Business is currently not compatible with windows based phones).
Once installed, open the app and sign on just as you would to FastNet Business, using your Client ID, User ID and password.
You’ll also be asked to enter the code on your Netcode token to complete the registration process.
You’ll then be able to view all your FastNet Business account balances and payments.

question: Can I change my FastNet Business password through the ASB Mobile Business app?
answer: Yes you can change your FastNet Business password in the settings menu. The app will also ask you to update your password on login if the current password is temporary or expired.

question: Can I deregister a Client ID from the ASB Mobile Business app?
answer: Yes, each Client ID can be deregistered from the app using the setting screen while you are logged in.

question: Is it possible to register multiple Client ID's on the ASB Mobile Business app?
answer:
Yes, users can now register up to 10 Clients ID’s on a single mobile device.
To register additional Client ID’s, log out of ASB Mobile Business and you will be presented with the log out screen and a number of options, select ‘Register Additional Client’. Follow the usual registration process for the new Client ID.
Once you have successfully completed the registration you will be presented with the login screen and the new Client ID dropdown menu. Tap on Client ID to select the site you wish to use. Tap done, enter the pin code that you set for the Client ID and you will have access to the regular menu in ASB Mobile Business.
A pin code is set for each Client ID and individual Client ID’s can be deregistered from the setting screen while you are logged in.

question: My phone is lost or has been stolen, what should I do in relation to my ASB Mobile Business app?
answer:
Please
let us know, by calling
0800 225 527
or
+ 64 9 623 8763
if you are calling  from overseas.
Please also Log In to FastNet Business and deregister your phone. To do this:
go into your User Preferences under the Administration menu (you don’t need Administrator approval to deregister your mobile)
OR
advise your FastNet Business administrator, so that they can deregister your phone on your behalf.

question: I have a new phone, how do I set up ASB Mobile Business?
answer:
Simply download the app and register your new phone. Because you can only operate ASB Mobile Business on one device at a time, you will be asked to accept the ‘deregistration’ of your old phone, please do this.
I’m having trouble registering my phone with ASB Mobile Business, what can I do?
Try uninstalling and downloading the app again, then try the registration process once more. If you are still having a problem, please contact the FastNet Business Help Desk on
0800 225 527.

question: Can I use ASB Mobile Business on my phone and tablet?
answer:
Not at the moment.
To ensure your security, we only allow one active device per user.  At this stage you can only register one FastNet Business Client ID on a device.

question: Can I get a new or replacement Clever Kash?
answer:
No. Clever Kash stopped being available to order on
15 September 2021
.
If your Clever Kash has been lost or broken, we won’t be able to replace it as there’s no remaining stock available.
If you're having trouble connecting Clever Kash to your ASB Mobile Banking app, follow our step‑by‑step troubleshooting guide:
I can’t connect my ASB Mobile Banking app to Clever Kash.

question: How can I help teach my child about money?
answer:
ASB is proud to have a legacy in helping kids get one step ahead with their money.
We introduced the GetWise financial literacy programme in 2010 and we’re pleased to be offering more kiwi kids taking out a new Headstart account, the chance to learn money-smart skills with some handy activity sheets to further educate kids about money and become cash clever.
Find out more about a
Headstart account
or the
GetWise programme
.

question: What information does the ASB Mobile Banking app access if installed on my device?
answer:
The ASB Mobile Banking app requires certain information/access permissions to work on your phone for the following services:
GPS location data:
This is used for the Branch & ATM Finder map functionality.
Identify phone call status:
This is used as we have a function allowing customers to call ASB from the app and to make sure the app works on incoming calls.
Read contact data of personal contacts:
